findings.recommendation: Treat as PROTOCOL-LOCKED: add a differential test (C#-generated block bytes with a duplicate tx and with a mismatched merkle root) asserting Rust `Block::deserialize` rejects them, or document explicitly that wire-level validation is intentionally deferred to neo-blockchain and ensure every deserialization entry point runs it. Also cap the var-int read at u16::MAX to mirror C#.

findings.detail: `Block::verify_merkle_root()` and `Block::verify_no_duplicate_transactions()` duplicate logic that already lives in neo-blockchain/src/block_validation.rs (validate_merkle_root / duplicate-hash check). A workspace grep shows the only caller of `verify_merkle_root` is tests/tests/csharp_compatibility_tests.rs:151 and `verify_no_duplicate_transactions` has no production caller. They are effectively dead helpers that risk drifting from the authoritative validation in neo-blockchain.
findings.recommendation: Either remove these methods and have the test call the neo-blockchain validators, or keep one canonical implementation and have both layers call it. Avoid two independent merkle/duplicate implementations that can diverge.

findings.detail: Doc comments still reference `neo-core` as the home of the full validation/verification helpers ("live in `neo-core`", "The full implementation is in `neo-core`"). Per project memory, neo-core was dissolved during the consolidation refactor; the helpers now live in neo-blockchain / neo-execution. These stale references mislead readers about where to find the real logic.
findings.recommendation: Update the doc comments to point at the actual current homes (neo-blockchain block_validation, neo-execution ApplicationEngine-based verification).

findings.detail: `ApplicationExecuted::script_hash()` has a docstring stating it "Returns the contract script hash of the executed call" but the body unconditionally `return None`. No external caller was found (the doc itself steers consumers to `notify.script_hash`). This is a documented-but-unimplemented accessor that will silently mislead any future caller.
findings.recommendation: Either implement it (derive from the relevant notification/trigger) or remove the method and the misleading docstring; if intentionally a no-op, state that explicitly.

findings.detail: `let state: Vec<StackItem> = self.state.iter().cloned().collect();` is an open-coded clone of the Vec; it allocates and clones element-by-element where `self.state.clone()` is clearer and at least as efficient.
findings.recommendation: Replace with `let state = self.state.clone();` (or pass `self.state.clone()` directly into `StackItem::from_array`).

per_crate.overall: A faithful, well-decomposed port of Neo.Plugins.OracleService. The consensus-relevant logic (signature aggregation threshold n-(n-1)/3, response-tx fee/size computation, multisig witness construction, finished/pending cache lifecycle, SyncPendingQueue set-difference) is a careful, correct mirror of the C# v3.9.1 reference, and in several spots is safer (returns errors where C# would NPE/panic on empty hash arrays or non-HALT verify). The crate is cleanly split one-type/one-fn-per-file, uses crate-internal thiserror error types per CONVENTIONS.md, and adds genuine value over C# via SSRF protection and request deduplication. The most impactful issues are not correctness bugs on the consensus path but rather (1) the whole HTTP/NeoFS fetching capability is gated behind an `oracle` feature that no consumer (neo-rpc, neo-node) ever enables, so the shipped node can verify/submit oracle signatures but cannot actually fetch any oracle data; (2) the crate declares `#![warn(missing_docs)]` yet leaves its entire public API (41 items) undocumented; (3) a dead/duplicated neofs branch and a no-op WalletChangedHandler. NeoFS error handling is uniformly lossy (every transport error collapses to a generic OracleResponseCode with the cause discarded), which hurts operability but not on-chain correctness.
strengths: Signature-aggregation and threshold logic in service/transactions/queue.rs (check_tx_sign, add_response_tx_sign) is a precise port of C# OracleService.AddResponseTxSign/CheckTxSign: threshold m = n-(n-1)/3, BTreeMap<ECPoint,_> gives the same key-ordered iteration as C#'s OrderBy(p=>p.Key), and the break-after-threshold sign emission matches.

create_response_tx (service/transactions/response.rs) faithfully reproduces the C# fee/size model: verification-engine FeeConsumed + execFeeFactor*MultiSignatureContractCost + per-byte fee, ResponseTooLarge/InsufficientFunds downgrade ordering, and witness-dict keyed by GetScriptHashesForVerifying ordering. Safer than C#: returns BuildFailed instead of null on non-HALT.
Defensive parity improvements over C#: empty script-hash array picks idx 1 instead of indexing [0] and panicking; verify-engine failure returns a typed error instead of null.

Genuine security hardening absent from the C# reference: SSRF validation (https/security.rs) with private/reserved IPv4+IPv6 ranges, IPv4-mapped IPv6 detection, octal-notation and credential-in-URL rejection, DNS-resolution internal-host check, redirect re-validation, streaming body size caps, and charset/content-type enforcement. Backed by unit tests.

Clean module decomposition (one struct/fn per file) and correct layered error strategy: crate-internal thiserror OracleServiceError at the boundary, String errors internally for the protocol fetch paths; no anyhow leakage.

ExpiringSet/ExpiryBoundary abstraction cleanly unifies the finished-cache (3-day, inclusive boundary to match C#'s next-sweep clearing) and dedup-cache (5-min, exclusive) TTL handling, with tests pinning the boundary semantics against the C# timer behavior.

findings.detail: All HTTP/NeoFS fetching lives behind the `oracle` feature (and `neofs-grpc`). default = []. The two consumers, neo-rpc (neo-rpc/Cargo.toml:28,114) and neo-node (neo-node/Cargo.toml:91), depend on the crate but never enable `oracle`/`neofs-grpc` (confirmed: no `neo-oracle-service/oracle` anywhere in the workspace). With the feature off, process_url has no `#[cfg(not(feature="oracle"))]` arm for the https branch, so an `https://` request falls through to ProtocolNotSupported. Net effect: the node ships an oracle service that can verify/submit signatures via RPC but cannot fetch any oracle data. The feature gating is either dead flexibility (if oracle fetching is intended to be off) or a wiring bug (if it is meant to be on).
findings.recommendation: Decide intent. If oracle fulfilment should work in the node, propagate the feature: add an `oracle`/`neofs-grpc` feature to neo-node (and neo-rpc) that enables `neo-oracle-service/oracle`. If fetching is intentionally optional, document that the default node cannot fulfil requests and ensure process_url returns a coherent code path when the feature is off. Either way add a build/test that exercises the feature-on path so it cannot silently rot.

findings.location: neo-oracle-service/src/service.rs:43-92; src/settings.rs:15-40; and 41 total items across the crate
findings.detail: The crate sets `#![warn(missing_docs)]` (lib.rs:2, per CONVENTIONS.md), yet `cargo build -p neo-oracle-service --features oracle` emits 41 missing-documentation warnings for this crate's own files: OracleStatus + its 3 variants, OracleServiceError + all 11 variants, every public field of OracleServiceSettings, and the public OracleService monitoring/dedup methods. Because the directive is `warn` not `deny`, the build passes and the gap is invisible in CI.
findings.recommendation: Add doc comments to the public enums/variants/fields/methods, or downgrade specific items. Given the project's documented DDD rule, documenting the public surface is the right fix. Consider `#![deny(missing_docs)]` once closed so regressions fail the build.

findings.detail: process_url handles `neofs:` twice. Lines 38-50 detect the prefix manually (`url[..6].eq_ignore_ascii_case("neofs:")`) and return self.neofs.process(...) before any parsing. Lines 53+ then parse the URL and lines 92-104 add a second `scheme.eq_ignore_ascii_case("neofs")` branch with identical body. Since url::Url::parse("neofs:...") yields scheme "neofs" but the first branch already returned, the second branch is unreachable dead code duplicating the first.
findings.recommendation: Remove the early prefix-match special case and rely on the parsed scheme dispatch (https/neofs/other), or remove the second branch. Collapse to a single scheme-based match after parsing so the two protocols are handled in one place.

findings.detail: WalletChangedHandler::wallet_provider_wallet_changed_handler initializes `wallet_arc: Option<Arc<dyn Wallet>> = None`, then attempts a downcast that only does `let _ = concrete;` (discards the result) and never assigns wallet_arc. Consequently `*self.wallet.write() = wallet_arc.clone()` always stores None, and the auto_start branch can never receive a wallet. The handler is effectively a no-op that clears the wallet regardless of input. It is documented as best-effort and currently has no production dispatcher (no caller outside the crate), so it is latent, but if/when wired it will silently fail to start the oracle on wallet-change.
findings.recommendation: Either implement the downcast properly (assign the concrete Arc<dyn Wallet> into wallet_arc) so auto-start works, or make the trait carry a concrete-enough type to avoid the Any round-trip. If it must stay a stub, return early / make the dead-on-input behavior explicit rather than silently overwriting the wallet with None.

findings.detail: Several NeoFS modules carry blanket `#![allow(dead_code)]`, suppressing dead-code detection across whole files (notably the JSON deserialization scaffolding which is a parallel path to the gRPC implementation). This masks which of the two NeoFS code paths are actually exercised and lets genuinely unused structs/fields accumulate undetected. settings.rs:105 `is_response_size_allowed` is also a pub method with no in-crate caller.
findings.recommendation: Replace file-wide allows with targeted `#[allow(dead_code)]` on the specific items that are intentionally part of a wire schema, or gate the JSON path behind its own cfg if it is not used when neofs-grpc is on. Remove or document genuinely unused public helpers like is_response_size_allowed.

findings.detail: Across the NeoFS path, transport/parse/verification errors are uniformly collapsed to `(OracleResponseCode::Error|Timeout, String::new())` via `Err(_) => ...`, discarding the underlying cause. C#'s ProcessUrlAsync surfaces the exception message into the response data string for diagnostics. Here an operator gets no signal on why a NeoFS fetch failed (DNS vs TLS vs signature-verify vs malformed object). This is observability, not consensus correctness (the on-chain code is what matters), but it makes field debugging of a designated oracle node hard.
findings.recommendation: Log (tracing::warn with the error) at each `Err(_)` site before mapping to a response code, or thread the error string into the returned data field on the non-success paths as C# does. At minimum add a debug log of the discarded error so failures are diagnosable.

findings.detail: Inside the per-pending-request loop, `self.wallet.read().clone()` is taken once per pending request, and for each wallet account the response_tx/backup_tx and their signatures are cloned (response_tx.clone(), backup_tx.clone(), tx_sign.clone()) to pass into add_response_tx_sign and send_response_signature. C# reads the wallet/accounts the same way, so this is parity, but the repeated full-Transaction clones per account on the processing path are avoidable. Low frequency (only designated oracle nodes, only on new requests).
findings.recommendation: Hoist the wallet read outside the inner loop and reuse a single Arc/borrow; pass transactions by reference into add_response_tx_sign where ownership is not actually required, cloning only at the genuine storage boundary. Optional given the low call rate.

findings.detail: add_response_tx_sign performs the MAX_PENDING_QUEUE_SIZE check under one lock acquisition (lines 34-40), releases it, then re-acquires the pending_queue lock (line 42). Between the two locks another thread can insert, so the capacity guard is a TOCTOU: the limit can be exceeded by concurrent callers. In practice contention is low (one timer loop + one request loop + RPC submissions), so overshoot is bounded, and the check at line 35 re-validates contains_key, but the size guarantee is not strict.
findings.recommendation: Acquire the lock once and perform the capacity check and the entry insertion within the same critical section, rather than locking twice.

per_crate.overall: A well-structured, command-shaped service crate that faithfully mirrors C# Blockchain.Persist semantics (per-block atomicity via a staged child DataCache, per-tx HALT/FAULT commit, C#-exact ledger record bytes, witness verification with the correct 3-GAS block cap / 0.06-GAS extensible cap). Documentation is excellent and the protocol-critical paths are heavily and convincingly tested with byte-level pins and end-to-end witness tests. The most impactful issues are not correctness bugs on the protocol path but (1) an unbounded in-memory LedgerContext that duplicates the entire chain in RAM with no eviction (OOM/DoS risk on a synced node), (2) a large block_validation.rs module that is ~70% dead code (only 4 of 13 validators wired in; the timestamp/primary-index validators are silently not applied), and (3) speculative dead abstractions (AsyncSystemContext trait, several legacy handle shims). Error handling on the verification path correctly fails closed and avoids panics on untrusted input.
strengths: Per-block atomicity in native_persist.rs is correct and well-documented: the whole OnPersist/tx/PostPersist sequence is staged in a child DataCache (snapshot.clone_cache()) and only commit()ed on full success, with per-tx child caches committed only on HALT — matches C# snapshot.Commit() semantics (native_persist.rs:263-403).

Consensus-witness verification is implemented with the correct distinct gas caps: 3-GAS (300_000_000) for block/header Header.Verify and 0.06-GAS for ExtensiblePayload, with the extensible whitelist built from committee + next-block-validators BFT + per-validator sig hash + state-validators (handlers.rs:147,384,464-543). End-to-end accept/reject tests over real secp256r1 signatures guard this (handlers.rs:1096-1351).

ledger_records.rs writes C#-exact key/value bytes (big-endian block index keys, TrimmedBlock ISerializable bytes, BinarySerializer stack-item TransactionState/HashIndexState) and pins them with an independent byte-layout test, not just a round-trip (ledger_records.rs:288-384). This is exactly the right discipline for protocol-locked storage bytes.

Verification on untrusted input fails closed without panicking: try_hash errors are logged and converted to VerifyResult::Invalid / early returns rather than unwrap (handlers.rs:546-570,697-708); store-less contexts fall back to an empty DataCache that fails state-dependent checks closed (handlers.rs:679-685).

The unverified-block park cache is bounded (MAX_UNVERIFIED_CACHE_SIZE=20_000) and drained in bounded batches (DRAIN_BATCH_SIZE=50), preventing an out-of-order-block flood from exhausting memory or starving the command loop (block_processing.rs:22-23,102-129).

genesis_block / bft_address / chain_state_initialized are pinned against the real mainnet genesis hash and C# create-genesis semantics (native_persist.rs:742-764), and the genesis re-init guard is double-probed (Ledger Prefix_Block OR NeoToken committee cache) so stores persisted before ledger records still report initialized.

findings.detail: LedgerContext accumulates the ENTIRE chain in RAM with no eviction: blocks_by_hash (full Block bodies), hashes_by_index (32B/block, resized to index+1) and headers_by_index (full Header per block) all grow monotonically as insert_block is called for every persisted block (handlers.rs:58,201,409). On the live durable-store path each block is also written to disk and RPC reads blocks back from the durable store via the LedgerContract readers (neo-rpc ledger_queries.rs), so this is unbounded duplication of the full chain in memory. Syncing mainnet (millions of blocks) will OOM the node; an attacker feeding blocks accelerates it. insert_block also clones the whole Block before insertion (handlers.rs:58 `(*block).clone()`).
findings.recommendation: Bound the in-memory caches (LRU/ring of the last N blocks/headers, or store only height->hash and resolve bodies from the durable store on demand). At minimum cap blocks_by_hash and reuse the existing lru dependency. Treat the durable LedgerContract store as the source of truth for cold reads.

findings.detail: block_validation.rs (719 LoC) exports 13 pub validators and a 13-variant BlockValidationError, but a workspace grep shows only validate_transaction_count_raw_with_limit, validate_merkle_root, validate_no_duplicate_transactions and validate_block_version are ever called (all from handlers.rs). validate_block_size(_raw), validate_transaction_count(_raw), validate_timestamp_bounds, validate_timestamp_progression, validate_witness_scripts, validate_primary_index, and BlockValidationError itself have zero external uses; variants HeaderValidationFailed/EmptyTransactionList/TransactionVerificationFailed are never constructed. The BlockLike-generic wrappers add a trait dependency for functions nobody calls.
findings.recommendation: Delete the dead validators and unused error variants, or wire the timestamp/primary-index validators into the persist path (see next finding). Keep only the 4 functions actually used plus their error variants; this removes ~half the module.

findings.detail: persist_next_expected_block re-implements timestamp-progression and primary-index checks inline (handlers.rs:366 `primary_index >= validators_count`, :377 `timestamp <= prev.timestamp`) instead of calling the existing validate_primary_index / validate_timestamp_progression in block_validation.rs, which do exactly this. The result is two parallel copies of the same rules: the module's versions are dead code while the live rules are open-coded. handle_headers (handlers.rs:137-145) makes a third inline copy of the same primary/prev-hash/timestamp triad.
findings.recommendation: Either route the inline checks through the block_validation helpers (single source of truth) or delete the duplicated helpers. Factor the shared structural-header triad used by both handle_headers and persist_next_expected_block into one helper.

findings.detail: handle_import (the Import command / bulk-import path) calls persist_block_sequence directly WITHOUT the structural pre-checks (version, tx-count, merkle root, duplicate transactions) and WITHOUT the consensus-witness verification that persist_next_expected_block applies (handlers.rs:333-399). So a block arriving via Import is admitted on weaker checks than the same block arriving via InventoryBlock. If Import is only ever fed from trusted local files this is acceptable, but the asymmetry is undocumented and the verify:bool flag on the Import struct (import.rs:11) is ignored entirely.
findings.recommendation: Either route handle_import through persist_next_expected_block (so it shares the structural+witness checks, honoring Import.verify), or document explicitly that Import is a trusted offline-only path and remove the dead `verify` field.

findings.detail: The AsyncSystemContext trait (with async settings/current_height) has zero implementors and zero callers anywhere in the workspace (grep confirms). It pulls in async_trait for an abstraction that is never instantiated. The doc comment claims the service may use 'either the sync or the async flavour', but only the sync SystemContext is used.
findings.recommendation: Delete AsyncSystemContext (and the async_trait dependency if it becomes unused) until an actual async-store implementor exists.

findings.detail: headers_from_index takes a write lock (`self.headers_by_index.write()`) for a purely read-only operation that only clones headers into a Vec. This needlessly serialises all concurrent header reads (e.g. P2P GetHeaders serving) against each other. block_hashes_from on the same struct correctly uses read().
findings.recommendation: Change the lock acquisition to `self.headers_by_index.read()`.

findings.detail: handle_persist_completed binds the computed block hash to `_hash` (leading underscore = intentionally-unused convention) but then actually uses it at line 79 in the Imported event. The underscore prefix is misleading: it signals 'unused' to readers and lint, yet the value is load-bearing.
findings.recommendation: Rename `_hash` to `hash`.

findings.detail: handle.rs carries a layer of legacy actor-style back-compat shims that are no-ops or near-dead: raw_ref() returns &() via a static, RawRef = (), tell_from/tell_from_async/tell_async ignore their _sender arg and just forward to tell(), and shutdown() does `drop(self.cmd_tx.clone())` (cloning then immediately dropping a sender — a no-op that does NOT actually close the channel; the doc even admits there is no Shutdown variant). These document themselves as 'New code should not call it' but remain on the public API.
findings.recommendation: Audit callers; remove the shims that have no remaining callers (raw_ref, tell_from*, tell_async). Make shutdown() either a real Shutdown command or document it as advisory-only, since the clone-drop has no effect.

per_crate.overall: neo-tee is a self-contained TEE/SGX support crate (wallet sealing, fair-ordering mempool, attestation) that is only consumed by neo-node behind an optional `tee` feature. Code quality is generally good: it is well-documented at the module level, fails closed in strict SGX mode, uses HKDF domain separation, OsRng for nonces, AES-GCM with AAD binding, restrictive 0o600 file permissions in most paths, and has solid unit-test coverage with adversarial cases (tamper/replay/AAD). The crypto building blocks come from vetted crates (aes-gcm, hkdf, sha2, zeroize) and the secp256r1/hash primitives correctly route through neo-crypto. The most impactful issues are localized: a hand-rolled Merkle root that duplicates neo-crypto's MerkleTree, a dead-logic gas cap in FcfsWithGasCap with a latent i64 overflow, an inconsistent world-readable sealed-key file, and an incomplete zeroize. None of these are consensus- or protocol-byte-level concerns since neo-tee is off the ledger/P2P path, but the fair-ordering and key-handling gaps matter for the crate's stated security goals.
strengths: Fails closed correctly in strict SGX mode: derive_sgx_sealing_key and generate_sgx_report require verified DCAP quote evidence and report_data sealing-key binding before producing material (sgx.rs:128-135, service.rs:410-415); local/remote report verification returns false when unimplemented rather than true (report.rs:439-447).

Sound cryptographic hygiene: HKDF-SHA256 with salt + context domain separation (sealing.rs:67-91), OsRng for AES-GCM nonces (sealing.rs:119-122), AAD binds public_key+script_hash to sealed keys with explicit mismatch check (sealed_key.rs:65-74), and unseal returns Zeroizing<Vec<u8>> so plaintext keys self-zero (sealed_key.rs:62-81).

derive_secp256r1_key_from_sealing_key deliberately refuses to fall back to a constant key when all 16 probes fail, with a clear comment that a constant would collapse per-enclave key isolation (tee_mempool.rs:420-428) - good security reasoning.
Monotonic counter, machine-id, and wallet metadata files are all written with 0o600 on unix (runtime.rs:524-545, 572-590; tee_wallet.rs:315-325).
Strong, adversarial test coverage: tamper detection, AAD integrity, replay protection, double-init, fail-closed SGX, and SGX quote v3 offset parsing are all exercised.

findings.detail: FcfsWithGasCap is dead logic plus a latent panic. `let capped_fee = network_fee.min(network_fee * max_gas_multiplier as i64)` is a no-op for the intended case: when network_fee >= 0 and max_gas_multiplier >= 1, `network_fee * mult >= network_fee`, so `.min()` always returns the original network_fee and the gas cap never applies. Worse, `network_fee * max_gas_multiplier as i64` can overflow i64 for an attacker-chosen network_fee, panicking in debug builds (this value comes from untrusted transaction input via add_transaction). The policy's stated purpose (deprioritize transactions paying excessive fees to prevent gas wars) is not implemented at all.
findings.recommendation: Decide the intended semantics. If the goal is to cap the fee used as the ordering tiebreaker against a median, compute the cap relative to a reference (e.g. median network_fee) using saturating arithmetic: `network_fee.saturating_mul(max_gas_multiplier as i64)` and clamp against the reference. At minimum replace `*` with `saturating_mul` to remove the overflow panic, and add a test that demonstrates the cap actually changes ordering.

findings.detail: compute_merkle_root is a hand-rolled single-SHA256 Merkle tree that duplicates neo-crypto's MerkleTree::compute_root (neo-crypto/src/merkle_tree.rs:92-117), which is already a workspace dependency. The hand-rolled version reimplements the same odd-node-duplication reduction loop. Maintaining a second Merkle implementation invites drift, and this one is single-SHA256 while neo-crypto's hash_pair convention differs - so the ordering-proof root is silently a different construction than the rest of the codebase.
findings.recommendation: Replace the body with neo_crypto::MerkleTree::compute_root over the ordered hashes (mapping [u8;32] to UInt256), returning [0u8;32] for the empty case to preserve current behavior. This removes ~28 lines and centralizes the construction. Note: the duplication-of-odd-leaf pattern (CVE-2012-2459) exists in both; since this is a TEE ordering proof (not a consensus root), it needs a differential test rather than a behavior change, but consolidating first reduces the surface.

findings.detail: SealedKey::save_to_file writes the sealed-key JSON with std::fs::write, which creates the file with default (typically world-readable, 0o644) permissions. Every other sensitive artifact in this crate is deliberately written with 0o600 (counter at runtime.rs:572-590, machine_id at runtime.rs:524-545, wallet metadata at tee_wallet.rs:315-325). While the key material inside is AES-GCM sealed, leaving the on-disk key container world-readable is an inconsistency that weakens defense-in-depth (e.g. ciphertext/nonce/counter exposure aids offline analysis) and contradicts the crate's own permission discipline.
findings.recommendation: Mirror the 0o600 OpenOptions pattern already used in save_metadata/save_monotonic_counter for sealed-key files (unix), with the non-unix std::fs::write fallback.

findings.detail: The zeroize-after-use step zeroes a copy, not the live key. `let mut key_copy = derived_key; key_copy.zeroize();` copies the [u8;32] (Copy type) into key_copy and zeroes only that copy; the original `derived_key` local remains in memory until it falls out of scope un-zeroed (and the cipher also holds an internal copy). The comment 'Zeroize derived key after use' overstates what happens. The same pattern appears in both seal_data_with_context and unseal_data.
findings.recommendation: Make derived_key itself zeroize on drop, e.g. wrap it in zeroize::Zeroizing<[u8;32]> at the point of derivation (derive_sealing_key returns [u8;32]; bind it as `let derived_key = Zeroizing::new(derive_sealing_key(...)?);`) and drop the manual key_copy dance. This actually zeroes the value used and removes the misleading copy.

findings.detail: `anyhow` is declared as a dependency but is never referenced anywhere in src/ (verified: no `anyhow` usage). CONVENTIONS.md restricts anyhow to neo-node, so an unused anyhow dep here is dead weight that also blurs the layered-error policy. Separately, aes-gcm (0.10), hkdf (0.12), and libloading (0.8) are pinned with literal version strings rather than `workspace = true`, while peer crypto deps (zeroize, sha2, k256, p256) are centralized in [workspace.dependencies]; this risks version drift across crates for the AEAD/KDF stack.
findings.recommendation: Remove the unused anyhow dependency. Promote aes-gcm/hkdf (and ideally libloading) into [workspace.dependencies] and reference them with `workspace = true` so the crypto stack is version-aligned workspace-wide.

findings.detail: InitResult is a public struct (re-exported via enclave::mod) with four public fields (state, sealing_key_derived, counter_loaded, hardware_attestation_available) and no field-level doc comments. The crate sets #![warn(missing_docs)]; public struct fields are subject to that lint. Either the warning is being tolerated or the lint does not fire on these - either way the public API is under-documented. Relatedly, initialize() always reports `sealing_key_derived: true` (line 233) because a derivation failure returns Err earlier, so the field is structurally always-true and its meaning is unclear to a caller.
findings.recommendation: Add doc comments to each InitResult field. Consider dropping the always-true sealing_key_derived field or documenting that it is invariantly true on the Ok path.

findings.detail: The bytes64 and bytes16 serde helper modules are near-identical copies differing only in the array length (64 vs 16) and the error message. This is ~85 lines of duplicated visitor/serialize/deserialize boilerplate for fixed-size hex byte arrays.
findings.recommendation: Collapse into one generic helper over [u8; N] (a const-generic Visitor and serialize/deserialize fns), or use an existing crate such as serde_with's `serde_as` with `Hex` / `Bytes` to handle fixed-size hex arrays. Either removes the second copy.

per_crate.overall: A well-structured, heavily-documented reth-style P2P host. The connection lifecycle (LocalNodeService accept/dial, per-peer RemoteNodeService state machine, PeerRegistry admission control) is clean, the handshake ordering mirrors C# faithfully, and the handle-side peer fold is correct with good race-condition test coverage. Wire framing/codec is bounded and parity-checked. The most impactful issues are not protocol bugs but operational/maintenance ones: (1) broadcast fan-out runs synchronously inside the single command loop awaiting per-peer bounded channels, so one stalled peer can stall all network commands (liveness/DoS); (2) a substantial amount of dead/parallel scaffolding (the entire TaskManagerService, the typed ProtocolMessage/NetworkMessage wire layer) that is exported but never used by the real hot path; and (3) stale module-level documentation describing dependencies and re-exports that no longer exist. None of the protocol-locked byte-level behavior appears wrong.
strengths: Handshake state machine in remote_node.rs (on_message, lines 763-1162) faithfully enforces C# ordering: version-first, then verack, repeated handshake commands are protocol violations, and the single-publisher teardown guarantee (run(), lines 446-465) means a peer can never outlive its connection.

PeerRegistry (peer_registry.rs) is the single source of truth for connected peers with C#-faithful admission control (total cap, per-IP cap, duplicate version-nonce filter) and strong unit-test coverage for each rule including per-address count decrement on remove.

Inbound payloads are bounded against DoS: codec caps a frame at PAYLOAD_MAX_SIZE (32 MiB) and InvPayload deserialize caps hashes at MAX_HASHES_COUNT (500), so the Inv/GetData serve loops are bounded; block-sync look-ahead is bounded to 500 in-flight with stall-detection rewind (request_blocks_if_behind, lines 690-759).

The handle-side peer fold (handle.rs PeerTracker) correctly resolves the phantom-resurrect race (record_peer_address only updates existing entries after folding pending events) and is covered by explicit tests for both interleavings.
Compression handling in message.rs matches C# Message.Create exactly (strictly > CompressionMinSize, must save more than CompressionThreshold), with boundary and incompressible-payload tests.
No reachable unwrap/expect/panic on untrusted input in the protocol path; all peer-supplied deserialization maps cleanly to a CloseReason::ProtocolViolation that tears the connection down.

findings.detail: All broadcast handlers fan out synchronously inside the single LocalNodeService command loop: `for (peer_id, handle) in self.registry.handles() { handle.send_*(...).await }`. Each send awaits a per-peer bounded mpsc channel (capacity 64, remote_node.rs:345). If any peer's per-peer task is slow to drain (slow socket, backpressure on framed.send), that peer's channel fills and the `.await` blocks the entire command loop — stalling new Start/ConnectPeer/DisconnectPeer/SetBlockHeight commands and every other peer's broadcast. A single slow or malicious peer thus degrades whole-node liveness and block/consensus relay. C# isolates per-RemoteNode mailboxes so one slow peer cannot head-of-line-block others.
findings.recommendation: Use try_send for broadcast fan-out (dropping to the slow peer rather than blocking everyone, matching a bounded relay), or fan out via tokio::spawn / FuturesUnordered so a slow peer cannot stall the command loop. At minimum, replace the awaited send with try_send and log+drop on Full for the broadcast paths.

findings.location: neo-network/src/task_manager.rs (whole file, 315 LoC) and re-exports in lib.rs:136-138
findings.detail: TaskManagerService/TaskManagerHandle/TaskManagerCommand/SyncTask is a complete command/handle/service skeleton whose handlers are no-ops (handle_add_task just inserts into a HashMap; register_peer/peers/shutdown carry #[allow(dead_code)]). The real block-sync logic lives entirely in remote_node.rs::request_blocks_if_behind. A grep shows TaskManagerService is referenced only by its own integration test, never by production code. The module's own doc admits 'The port of the actual scheduling logic is deferred.' This is ~315 lines of exported public API that does nothing and risks confusing consumers into wiring a dead orchestrator.
findings.recommendation: Either delete the task_manager module and its re-exports until the scheduling logic is actually ported, or downgrade it to a private/internal placeholder not in the public API. Keeping a fully-public no-op orchestrator alongside the real per-peer sync invites misuse.

findings.location: neo-network/src/wire/protocol_message.rs (259 LoC) + network_message.rs (122 LoC); re-exported lib.rs:139-142
findings.detail: ProtocolMessage (a strongly-typed per-command payload enum) and NetworkMessage (header+flags+typed payload envelope) implement a full second decode/encode path for every P2P command, but the production hot path in remote_node.rs never uses them — on_message decodes the raw `Message` and manually calls e.g. Block::deserialize / VersionPayload::deserialize per command arm. grep confirms ProtocolMessage/NetworkMessage are used nowhere outside their own modules and the lib re-export. This is a parallel, unused wire abstraction (~380 LoC) duplicating the dispatch logic, increasing maintenance surface and risking divergence from the path that actually runs.
findings.recommendation: Either adopt ProtocolMessage in remote_node.rs::on_message (replacing the manual per-command MemoryReader/deserialize arms with a single ProtocolMessage::deserialize_payload dispatch) to eliminate the duplicate dispatch, or remove the typed layer if the manual path is preferred. Do not keep both.

findings.detail: Module-level documentation is stale and misleading. It states the crate depends on 'neo-blockchain / neo-mempool (Layer 2)' and 'neo-core (legacy) — re-exports of the actor types' and devotes a whole 'Back-compat (Stage F will remove)' section to 're-exported legacy Akka-style actor types' mapping neo_core::network::p2p::* to new types. Cargo.toml contains none of neo-blockchain, neo-mempool, or neo-core; the only neo-p2p re-export is ChannelsConfig (a config struct, not an actor). The doc describes an architecture that no longer exists post-consolidation.
findings.recommendation: Update the lib.rs crate docs to reflect the actual dependency set (neo-primitives/neo-error/neo-config/neo-io/neo-payloads/neo-runtime/neo-p2p) and delete the obsolete 'Back-compat (Stage F)' / legacy-actor-re-export sections.

findings.detail: `#[allow(dead_code)] fn _force_reply_link(_r: oneshot::Sender<NetworkResult<()>>) {}` is a vestigial no-op kept solely (per its name) to force a use of the oneshot type. `oneshot` is already used throughout the file (handle_start/connect/disconnect use oneshot reply channels), so this linker hack serves no purpose.
findings.recommendation: Delete the dead `_force_reply_link` function.

findings.detail: `WireError::InvalidMessage(format!("invalid flags byte"))` calls format! with a constant string and no interpolation — a clippy::useless_format lint, and it discards the actual invalid byte value that would aid debugging.
findings.recommendation: Use a plain string and include the offending byte, e.g. `WireError::InvalidMessage(format!("invalid flags byte: 0x{byte:02x}"))` after binding the read byte, or `.to_string()` if the value is not retained.

findings.detail: The node identity nonce — used for self-connection detection (remote_node.rs:1206) and reported by RPC getversion — is derived as `RandomState::new().build_hasher().finish() as u32`. This relies on the unspecified internal behavior of std's SipHash RandomState (the value of finish() on an un-fed hasher), which is not a documented or stable source of randomness and is only 32 bits truncated from a 64-bit hash seed. It happens to vary per process today but is a fragile way to obtain entropy for a protocol-visible identity.
findings.recommendation: Source the nonce from an explicit RNG (the workspace already depends on rand-family crates elsewhere) or from neo-crypto's randomness, rather than relying on the undocumented output of an unfed SipHasher. Flag as needs-differential-test if changing, since the nonce is protocol-visible.

findings.detail: PeerId and TaskId both derive Default (yielding the 0 value) while also providing a `new()` that allocates from an atomic counter starting at 1. The two constructors disagree: Default produces id 0 (never allocated by new()), and `new()` does not go through Default. The integration test even relies on `TaskId::default()` being distinct from an allocated id. Deriving Default on a monotonic-id newtype is a mild footgun (e.g. a Default PeerId could collide with nothing but is semantically a 'null' peer).
findings.recommendation: Either drop the Default derive on these id newtypes (forcing explicit construction), or document that Default is the reserved sentinel 0 and never collides with new(). Prefer removing Default unless a sentinel is genuinely needed.

per_crate.overall: A well-organized, one-type-per-file protocol crate that faithfully mirrors the C# Neo.SmartContract.Manifest / NefFile types. The stack-value (VM interop) projections are thoroughly tested and a known consensus-critical bug (C# JavaScriptEncoder escape semantics for manifest `extra`) is explicitly guarded with a regression test. Layering is clean (no upward deps) and the lint directives required by CONVENTIONS.md are present. The most impactful issues are parity gaps against the C# reference rather than structural problems: NefFile deserialization omits the C# empty-script rejection, ContractAbi::get_method returns the wrong overload for the pcount=-1 (any-arity) lookup used by contract `verify` resolution, and several public APIs swallow malformed input where C# throws. There is also a sizeable parallel hand-rolled JSON path that duplicates the serde derive and can drift from it. None are confirmed live consensus breaks, but the NEF and get_method items are reachable on the deploy/verify paths and warrant differential tests + fixes.
strengths: The `extra`/features C# JavaScriptEncoder.Default escape handling (contract_manifest.rs:500-547) routes through neo_serialization::JsonSerializer::encode_value_csharp_compatible and is pinned by the Bug #10 regression test (contract_manifest.rs:718-799). This is consensus-critical and must not be reverted to plain serde_json.

Stack-value <-> stack-item projections for every manifest type have paired round-trip tests asserting to_stack_item() == StackItem::try_from(to_stack_value()); the layouts match the C# ToStackItem field order (manifest Struct of 8, ABI of 2, etc.). Do not reorder these.
Clean one-type-per-file module layout, full #![deny(unsafe_code)] / #![warn(missing_docs)] (lib.rs:41-42), and correct downward-only layering with no neo-core/neo-execution dependency.
NefFile checksum is computed and verified on deserialize against the C# Hash256(..^4) algorithm (nef_file.rs:80-102,259-266), and serialize enforces compiler/source/token/script caps.

Group-signature-against-hash validation is correctly delegated to the consumer (neo-native-contracts contract_management.rs:472-487 manifest_is_valid), matching C# IsValid(limits, hash) including the BinarySerializer limits check; the manifest crate intentionally stays hash-agnostic.

findings.detail: NefFile::deserialize diverges from C# NefFile.Deserialize (neo_csharp/.../NefFile.cs:124-141) in two ways. (1) C# rejects an empty script: `if (Script.Length == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Script cannot be empty.")`; the Rust path has no such check, so a NEF with valid magic+checksum but zero-length script is accepted. The consumer parse_nef_checked (contract_management.rs:353-362) only guards the OUTER payload length, not the inner script, so this gap is reachable on the deploy/update path. (2) C# reads the script with `MaxItemSize` as the cap and then verifies the total parsed size <= MaxItemSize; Rust reads with u32::MAX (nef_file.rs:248) and performs no total-size check on read (the MaxItemSize check exists only on the serialize side, nef_file.rs:200-207).
findings.recommendation: In deserialize, read script with `neo_vm_rs::ExecutionEngineLimits::DEFAULT.max_item_size` as the cap, reject `script.is_empty()` with an invalid_data error, and (matching C# verify=true) reject when total bytes read exceed MaxItemSize. Add a differential test against C# Parse for empty-script and oversized-script NEFs — protocol-locked wire behavior, verify with a fixture, do not just refactor.

findings.detail: For the pcount==-1 (any-arity) lookup, C# returns Methods.FirstOrDefault(p => p.Name == name) i.e. the FIRST method with that name (ContractAbi.cs:96-98). The Rust version pre-builds a HashMap inserting `(name, -1) => i` for every method in order (line 45), so a later duplicate-name method overwrites the earlier index and the lookup returns the LAST overload. This path is reachable: VERIFY_P_COUNT == -1 (contract_basic_method.rs:30) and is used to resolve a contract's `verify` method in neo-execution helper.rs:264-271 and neo-oracle-service response.rs:109-113. A contract declaring multiple `verify` overloads (legal in the ABI) would resolve to a different method than the C# node.
findings.recommendation: Match C# exactly: for pcount < 0 use `self.methods.iter().find(|m| m.name == name)` (first match) and only build/use the (name, len) dictionary for pcount >= 0. Add a differential test with two same-named overloads and pcount=-1.

findings.detail: Multiple stack-item decode paths silently drop or ignore malformed data where C# FromStackItem throws. ContractManifest::from_stack_value uses filter_map(...ok()) for groups (line 575) and trusts (line 637), silently skipping un-decodable entries instead of erroring; C# does `((Array)@struct[1]).Select(p => p.ToInteroperable<ContractGroup>())` which throws. ContractGroup's Interoperable::from_stack_item (contract_group.rs:191-196) catches a decode error, logs it, and returns Ok(()) leaving *self unchanged — a contract group that fails to parse is silently treated as the prior value. parse_extra_bytes returns None on invalid JSON (lines 697-703) whereas C# `JToken.Parse` throws. These decode from the storage stack-item form of a ContractState; silent divergence can produce a manifest that does not round-trip identically to what C# would reconstruct.
findings.recommendation: Propagate decode errors (collect into Result instead of filter_map(...ok())) and make ContractGroup::from_stack_item return the error rather than swallowing it, so behavior matches C#'s throw-on-malformed. Gate with a round-trip/differential test on a stored ContractState.

findings.location: neo-manifest/src/manifest/contract_abi.rs:84-120; contract_method_descriptor.rs:80-121; contract_event_descriptor.rs:40-68; contract_parameter_definition.rs:36-61; method_token.rs:76-111; wild_card_container.rs:55-81; contract_permission_descriptor.rs:64-91
findings.detail: Every descriptor type derives Serialize/Deserialize AND also carries hand-written to_json()/from_json() methods that re-encode the same JSON shape. The two paths can drift. The hand-written from_json paths additionally use filter_map(...ok()) (contract_abi.rs:92,102; contract_event_descriptor.rs:54; contract_method_descriptor.rs:94) which silently drops malformed methods/events/parameters, unlike both the serde path and C# FromJson (which throws). A grep shows the descriptor-level to_json/from_json are not used by external consumers (RPC uses its own Rpc* models); they are effectively a parallel, partially-incorrect serializer kept alive only by internal recursion and tests.
findings.recommendation: Delete the hand-rolled to_json/from_json on the descriptor types and route through serde_json::to_value/from_value (the derives already exist and use the correct rename/Serialize impls). If a couple must stay for ergonomics, implement them in terms of serde rather than rebuilding the JSON by hand.

findings.location: neo-manifest/src/method_token.rs:53-111; nef_file.rs:132-151; contract_method_descriptor.rs:47-110; contract_event_descriptor.rs:23-60; contract_parameter_definition.rs:23-53; contract_abi.rs:84; wild_card_container.rs:68,123; contract_permission_descriptor.rs:64,138
findings.detail: CONVENTIONS.md mandates CoreError/CoreResult for errors crossing crate boundaries (library crates must use typed errors). These public, cross-boundary constructors/parsers return Result<_, String> (stringly-typed) instead of CoreResult, while sibling APIs in the same crate (ContractManifest::validate, ContractGroup::validate/verify_signature) correctly use CoreResult. The mixed convention is inconsistent and forces callers to map String into CoreError ad hoc (e.g. contract_management.rs:359,387 do `.map_err(|e| CoreError::invalid_operation(...))`).
findings.recommendation: Migrate the public String-returning APIs to CoreResult<T> for boundary-crossing functions to match the layered error strategy; keep String only for genuinely internal helpers. Low priority but improves consistency and removes per-callsite error mapping.

findings.detail: Both module-level doc comments are stale: they state the manifest types 'remain in neo_core::smart_contract::manifest' / that neo_core 'continues to be the canonical home', and describe a future refactor to move them out of neo-core. The types now actually live in this crate (the crate description in Cargo.toml correctly calls neo-manifest the 'Canonical home'). The docs describe a no-longer-existent state and reference neo-core which the crate must not depend on.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the Status/Layering doc blocks to reflect that the full manifest::* types now live here; drop the references to neo_core being the canonical home and the pending-extraction language.

findings.detail: The method is documented as matching C# ContractManifest.Parse 'exactly' (line 175), but C# Parse(ReadOnlySpan<byte>) first rejects input whose byte length exceeds MaxLength=u16::MAX before parsing (ContractManifest.cs:143-148). The Rust parse/from_json_str performs no length guard; the size cap is only enforced later in validate() via the recomputed binary size(). The deploy path mitigates this by checking raw byte length in contract_management.rs:375, but a direct caller of ContractManifest::parse gets no bound and the 'matches C# exactly' claim is inaccurate.
findings.recommendation: Either add a `json.len() > MAX_MANIFEST_LENGTH` guard inside parse to genuinely match C# Parse, or soften the doc comment to note the length check is the caller's responsibility.

per_crate.overall: A focused, mostly well-built crate. The compression helpers, the C#-compatible JSON escaping (CSharpEscapeFormatter), the JToken model, and the binary stack-item codec are carefully implemented and well-tested against C# v3.x vectors, with thorough doc comments explaining parity rationale and a clean separation between consensus-relevant Neo binary format and the Rust-internal bincode/serde diagnostic helpers. The dominant problem is the hand-rolled JSONPath evaluator (json/j_path_token.rs): it is a strict, incomplete subset of C# Neo.Json.JPathToken that (a) omits the C# DoS bounds (maxDepth=6, maxObjects=1024) and (b) diverges in supported grammar, and it sits on a consensus-relevant, untrusted-input path (Oracle request filter). There is also a secondary parity concern: the two binary deserializers use inconsistent item-count accounting, one faithful to C# and one not. The remaining items are style/clarity nits.
strengths: json/escape.rs CSharpEscapeFormatter is an exemplary, well-documented, well-tested reproduction of System.Text.Json JavaScriptEncoder.Default (quote as ", HTML-sensitive ASCII, UTF-16 surrogate pairs, uppercase hex) - do not break this.

BinarySerializer uses an explicit work-queue (Vec/VecDeque) instead of recursion for both serialize and deserialize, and detects circular references via an identity HashSet - DoS-resistant and matches C#'s iterative algorithm. The map insertion-order round-trip is verified by deserialize_preserves_map_entry_order_for_roundtrip_bytes and the NEP-17 mainnet-divergence regression test.

serialization.rs clearly documents (module header + per-fn) that bincode/serde_json helpers are Rust-internal/diagnostic and MUST NOT persist consensus-relevant state, while only the *_neo_binary helpers produce the C#-compatible format - prevents a dangerous misuse.

JsonSerializer carefully reproduces C# StdLib.jsonSerialize/Deserialize semantics: JS safe-integer range check, fractional-number rejection, BigInteger.Parse(double.ToString()) path, per-item and per-map-entry maxStackSize charging, with explanatory comments and C#-vector tests.
JToken deserialization (TokenSeed/TokenVisitor) bounds nesting depth to prevent serde_json stack overflow on adversarial input and rejects duplicate object keys, matching C#.

findings.detail: The JSONPath evaluator is a hand-rolled, incomplete reimplementation of C# Neo.Json.JPathToken. C# (neo_csharp/src/Neo.Json/JPathToken.cs ProcessJsonPath) supports recursive descent ($..name), array slices ($[start:end]), unions ($[1,2] / $['a','b']), negative indices, and quoted-key access ($['key']). The Rust evaluate only handles $, ., .ident, [number], and [*] - it returns an error for the other forms (bracket String/Colon/Comma cases fall through to 'Unsupported bracket expression'). It also lacks the C# DoS bounds maxDepth=6 and maxObjects=1024 enforced on every Descent. This is reached from neo-oracle-service/src/service/utils.rs:36 (filter_json -> json_path) where the filter string comes from the on-chain OracleRequest (untrusted). A filter valid in C# but rejected/expanded differently in Rust yields a different Oracle response committed on-chain -> consensus divergence; the missing maxObjects cap is also a DoS amplifier. Additionally parse_string has an index bug: for input like $['a'] the parse loop returns content length excluding quotes then re-advances onto the closing quote, double-counting it (C# ParseString includes both quotes and the loop does i += Content.Length - 1).
findings.recommendation: Treat as a protocol-parity item requiring a differential test against C# JPathToken before any change. Port the full C# grammar (ProcessDot/ProcessBracket/ProcessSlice/ProcessUnion/recursive descent, negative indices, quoted keys) and the maxDepth=6/maxObjects=1024 guards, fix the parse_string quote accounting, and add fixtures generated from the C# implementation. Until then, document the supported subset and the consensus risk for Oracle filters.

findings.detail: The two binary deserializers count items differently. deserialize/deserialize_with_limits pre-projects child counts (Array: total_items += count + 1; Map: total_items += count*2 + 1) and pre-checks count against the remaining budget, whereas the sibling deserialize_stack_value_with_limits increments total_items += 1 once per loop iteration - which matches C# BinarySerializer.Deserialize (neo_csharp/src/Neo/SmartContract/BinarySerializer.cs:83-128, which only checks deserialized.Count > maxItems after each single push and never pre-checks count). Because deserialize feeds StdLib.deserialize and native-contract state decoding (consensus-critical), an over-strict or differently-timed item cap can reject a payload C# accepts (or vice-versa) at the boundary. The unchecked remaining += count / count*2 in deserialize is also inconsistent with the checked_add in deserialize_stack_value (not currently reachable to overflow given the prior saturating_sub guard, but the divergence is a smell).
findings.recommendation: Add a differential test against C# at the maxItems/maxSize boundary for arrays and maps, then unify both deserializers on the C#-faithful per-push counting used by deserialize_stack_value. Protocol-locked: verify with a differential fixture rather than refactoring blindly.

findings.detail: BinarySerializer and JsonSerializer public APIs return Result<_, String> (stringly-typed errors), and binary_serializer.rs:507 has a private io_error_to_string converter. CONVENTIONS.md mandates layered errors: a crate-internal thiserror type for crate-local errors and neo_error::CoreError across crate boundaries. The crate already defines json::JsonError (thiserror) for the JToken side, so the String-based error in these two serializers is an inconsistency, and it discards the structured IoError variants from neo-io.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a crate-internal thiserror SerializerError (or reuse JsonError) for these APIs, mapping neo_io::IoError variants through #[from], and expose CoreError at the crate boundary per CONVENTIONS.md. Low priority since callers currently treat these as opaque fault strings.

findings.detail: JObject::children() allocates a fresh Vec<&Option<JToken>> on every call (collects the iterator). In JPathToken::evaluate the [*] wildcard branch calls object.children() inside a loop over results (j_path_token.rs:186-192), so each matched object triggers a heap allocation just to iterate its values.
findings.recommendation: Have children() return an iterator (e.g. impl Iterator<Item=&Option<JToken>>) or add a values() iterator and use it at the call site; keep the Vec-returning method only if an external API needs it. Minor given typical JSON sizes.

findings.detail: JArray::get is documented 'without bounds checking' but actually does bounds-check via Vec::get (returns None on OOB) - the doc is misleading. count() is a redundant alias of len() kept only to mirror C# naming; combined with len()/is_empty() it adds surface with no behavioral value.
findings.recommendation: Fix the get() doc comment to say it returns None when out of bounds. Consider dropping count() (or documenting it explicitly as a C#-parity alias) to reduce duplicated API surface.

findings.detail: Both files start with a module-level #![allow(clippy::mutable_key_type)] placed before the //! doc comment and the crate's lint directives, with no comment explaining why the lint is suppressed. The crate's HashSet<(usize, StackItemType)> identity keys are the reason (StackItem-derived), but a reader cannot tell.
findings.recommendation: Add a one-line // reason comment next to the allow explaining the identity-key usage, and confirm the allow is still required after the StackItemType-based keying (which is not a mutable-key type).

per_crate.overall: A focused, mostly clean wallet crate. The data model (Wallet trait, WalletAccount/StandardWalletAccount, Nep6Wallet) is uniform and well-documented, secret material is properly zeroized, and NEP-2 AES-256-ECB/Base58Check encoding plus the constant-time password check are correct and well-commented for C# parity. asset_descriptor.rs is an exemplary faithful port with strong tests. The single most impactful issue is a genuine interoperability correctness bug: the NEP-2 address-hash salt is derived from a hand-rolled verification script that uses the wrong syscall (System.Crypto.CheckWitness as a raw ASCII string) instead of the canonical System.Crypto.CheckSig 4-byte syscall hash, so NEP-2 keys produced/consumed here are not interoperable with C#/standard Neo wallets. The round-trip test passes only because the same wrong helper is used on both encrypt and decrypt, masking it. Secondary issues are mostly cleanliness: crate-wide allow(dead_code)/allow(unused_imports), Result<_,String> error handling in bip32/version/wallet_factory (against CONVENTIONS.md), a dead Arc<Nep6Wallet> field on every account, a never-populated Box<dyn Any> field, and a duplicated invocation-script builder that ignores the crate's own signature_invocation helper.
strengths: KeyPair zeroizes private material (zeroize(drop)), redacts the private key in Debug, and uses subtle::ConstantTimeEq for both the NEP-2 address-hash check (key_pair.rs:354) and KeyPair equality (key_pair.rs:402) — correct DoS/timing-oracle hygiene that must be preserved.

NEP-2 crypto matches C#: AES-256-ECB no-padding over the two 16-byte XOR blocks, Base58Check '6P...' encoding, scrypt N=16384/r=8/p=8, with detailed comments (key_pair.rs:266-285) explaining why ECB/Base58Check (not CBC/base64) is protocol-required. Do not 'modernize' these.

asset_descriptor.rs is a high-quality faithful port of C# Neo.Wallets.AssetDescriptor: correct 0.3 GAS probe budget, correct pop order (symbol on top, decimals below), rejects out-of-range decimals instead of silently wrapping (to_u8), and has good GAS/NEO/nonexistent-asset tests.
Sensitive intermediates (derived scrypt key, XOR key, decrypted bytes) are wrapped in Zeroizing throughout key_pair.rs; BIP-39 entropy and ExtendedKey private/chain-code are likewise zeroized.
Public API is well-documented at the trait/struct level; the Wallet/WalletAccount split is clean and the StandardWalletAccount delegation from Nep6Account is uniform.
wallet_helper::to_script_hash maps each Base58Check/address decode error to a specific, actionable message rather than swallowing it.

findings.detail: The NEP-2 address-hash salt is computed from a hand-rolled verification script that emits SYSCALL followed by the raw ASCII string b"System.Crypto.CheckWitness" (26 bytes). The canonical Neo signature redeem script (neo-vm/src/script_builder/redeem_script.rs:50 and KeyPair::get_verification_script at key_pair.rs:153 via Helper::signature_redeem_script) is `PUSHDATA1 0x21 <33-byte pubkey> SYSCALL <4-byte little-endian hash of System.Crypto.CheckSig>`. C# Neo (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Wallets/KeyPair.cs:124-126) derives the NEP-2 salt from `Contract.CreateSignatureRedeemScript(PublicKey).ToScriptHash()`. Because this helper uses the wrong syscall name (CheckWitness vs CheckSig) AND the wrong encoding (raw string vs 4-byte syscall hash), the script hash — and therefore the address used as the scrypt salt — differs from C#. Result: NEP-2 keys exported by this code are NOT importable by C#/standard wallets, and standard '6P...' keys fail the password check here even with the correct password. The test at key_pair.rs:450 passes only because the same wrong helper is used on both encrypt (:235) and decrypt (:347), so the round-trip self-consistently agrees while being globally wrong. KeyPair::get_verification_script (the correct one) and this helper compute different hashes for the same key.
findings.recommendation: Replace the body of try_get_verification_script_for_key with the canonical Helper::signature_redeem_script(&compressed) (the same call get_verification_script already uses), so the NEP-2 salt matches C#. Then add a differential/known-answer test using a published NEP-2 vector (e.g. the BIP-38/NEP-2 reference '6PYN...' string with its known WIF/address) to lock byte-level C# parity — this is protocol-locked behavior, so verify via fixture rather than trusting the internal round-trip.

findings.detail: Every Nep6Account carries `_wallet: Arc<Nep6Wallet>` that is written in three constructors but never read (grep confirms no reads). Worse, because Nep6Wallet derives Clone and shares `accounts: Arc<RwLock<HashMap>>`, each account stores Arc::new(wallet.clone()) which pins a handle to the wallet's entire account map. The mutation paths (change_password/unlock/lock/set_default_account) deep-clone accounts and rebuild the map, leaving each account's _wallet pointing at a stale pre-mutation snapshot — harmless only because it is dead. This is dead state plus a non-obvious aliasing cost.
findings.recommendation: Remove the _wallet field and the clones that populate it. If a back-reference is genuinely needed later, model it with a Weak<...> and read it; do not keep an unread underscore-prefixed field.

findings.location: neo-wallets/src/bip32/extended_key.rs (Result<_,String> throughout), bip32/key_path.rs:29, version.rs:28/55, wallet_factory.rs:28/37; lib.rs:37-38
findings.detail: CONVENTIONS.md mandates a layered error strategy (neo_error::CoreError/CoreResult across boundaries, crate-internal thiserror types internally). bip39.rs correctly defines a thiserror Bip39Error, but the bip32 module, version.rs, and the public WalletFactory trait all return Result<_, String>, stringly-typed errors that cannot be matched on by callers and lose structure. Additionally lib.rs carries crate-wide `#![allow(dead_code)]` and `#![allow(unused_imports)]` (lines 37-38), which directly contradict the `#![warn(missing_docs)]` discipline on line 36 and the production-ready/no-dead-code convention; these blanket allows hide exactly the kind of issues (e.g. the dead _wallet field, unused WalletProvider trait) this review found.
findings.recommendation: Introduce a thiserror Bip32Error (and reuse it for KeyPath) and migrate WalletFactory/Version to typed errors or CoreError. Remove the crate-wide allow(dead_code)/allow(unused_imports) and fix the few resulting warnings rather than suppressing them globally.

findings.detail: create_witness hand-builds the invocation script as `OpCode::PUSHDATA1.byte()` + `signature.len() as u8` + signature, while the crate already exports a canonical signature_invocation() helper (also re-exported from lib.rs:88) that does the same via ScriptBuilder::emit_push and additionally validates the 64-byte length. The hand-rolled version skips the length check and duplicates push logic. key_pair.rs:377-383 likewise hand-rolls a PUSHDATA1 push instead of using ScriptBuilder.
findings.recommendation: Have create_witness call signature_invocation(&signature)? so there is one place that encodes a signature push (and gains the 64-byte validation). Similarly route the pubkey push in the verification-script helper through ScriptBuilder.

findings.detail: TransferOutput.data is Option<Box<dyn Any>> mirroring C#'s `object Data`, but every caller (neo-rpc rpc_server_wallet/mod.rs:487, :746) sets it to None and nothing ever downcasts it. The dyn Any erases the type, is never read, and silently makes TransferOutput non-Clone/non-Send-friendly for no benefit. Separately, the neo-wallets WalletProvider trait (wallet_provider.rs) has no implementors in the workspace (the real ones live in neo-system / neo-payloads), and its wallet_changed() returns a fresh mpsc::Receiver per call — a literal C# event port that does not translate to idiomatic Rust.
findings.recommendation: Drop the dead `data` field (or, if NEP-17 transfer-data is actually needed, type it concretely, e.g. Option<Vec<u8>> / a ContractParameter). Consider removing the unused neo-wallets WalletProvider trait or document why it exists separately from neo-system's.

findings.detail: change_password returns Ok(true) immediately when old_password == new_password, without verifying the password is correct. C# NEP6Wallet.ChangePassword (neo_csharp/src/Neo/Wallets/NEP6/NEP6Wallet.cs:357-385) never short-circuits on equality; it always runs ChangePasswordPrepare (which verifies the old password) across all accounts. The Rust short-circuit means a caller can 'succeed' at a no-op change with an incorrect password, which is a subtle behavioral divergence (and could mask a wrong-password condition to callers that treat true as 'old password was valid').
findings.recommendation: Remove the old==new early return (or, if kept as an optimization, first verify old_password via verify_password before returning Ok(true)).

findings.detail: The From<String> bridge buckets any 'Invalid entropy length...' message into InvalidEntropyLength { got: 0 }, discarding the real bit count, and routes everything else into UnknownLanguage even when it is not a language problem (e.g. checksum mismatches reuse UnknownLanguage at :180-183). This is a legacy string-bridging artifact that produces misleading structured errors (got:0) and conflates distinct failure modes under one variant.
findings.recommendation: Remove the lossy From<String>/From<&str> bridges once callers no longer pass strings, and add a dedicated variant (e.g. InvalidMnemonic/ChecksumMismatch) instead of overloading UnknownLanguage for checksum/word-count failures.

per_crate.overall: neo-p2p is a small (~2654 LoC), well-structured "protocol types only" crate that is in good shape. It is clippy-clean (the only clippy warnings surfaced by an --all-targets build originate in neo-primitives, not here), follows the one-type-per-file convention, uses thiserror for its internal error type, and re-exports shared enums from neo-primitives rather than duplicating them. Parity with the vendored C# v3.9.1 reference is strong and deliberately documented: WitnessCondition binary/JSON nesting-depth handling, the ECPoint normalization for Group conditions, capability uniqueness checks, and the InvPayload cap-only-on-deserialize behaviour all match C# exactly, and there are good differential tests (uncompressed-ECPoint normalization, unknown-byte round-trips, stack-item projection). Bounded reads (deserialize_array capped + Vec::with_capacity(count<=max)) make it DoS-resistant. The findings below are quality/clarity nits and one log-spam DoS vector, not correctness or consensus bugs — there are no reachable panics on the untrusted-input path and no protocol-byte issues.
strengths: Strong, deliberately-documented C# parity: WitnessCondition nesting-depth decrement matches C# DeserializeFrom/DeserializeConditions exactly (serialization.rs:56-131 vs AndCondition.cs:62-66), and WitnessRule::from_json passes full MaxNestingDepth to the condition matching WitnessRule.cs FromJson.

Group condition correctly accepts both 33-byte compressed and 65-byte uncompressed ECPoints and normalizes to compressed, mirroring C# ECPoint.DecodePoint, with a dedicated differential test (serialization.rs:71-80, tests.rs:99-125). Breaking this would reject txs C# accepts.

DoS-resistant deserialization: all array reads are bounded by an explicit max with the count re-checked before allocation (helper.rs:79-90); var-bytes/var-string reads carry explicit caps (filter_load 36000, user_agent 1024, unknown-capability 1024).

message_flags is correctly backed by the bitflags crate and preserves unknown bits for forward-compatible round-trips rather than rejecting them (protocol_message_flags macro + tests at message_flags.rs:46-66) — must not be replaced with a hand-rolled byte.
Clean error layering and no reachable unwrap/panic on the untrusted path; the only non-test .expect() (stack_projection.rs:65,73) guards a genuinely-infallible internal invariant.
Good protocol regression tests pinning SINGLE_QUEUED/HIGH_PRIORITY command sets and the LZ4 compression whitelist against C# RemoteNode policy (message_command.rs:108-152).

findings.detail: WitnessCondition::len() returns size() (serialized byte count) and is_empty() returns size()==0. But size() is always `1 + payload` (witness_rule.rs:143), so it is never 0, meaning is_empty() ALWAYS returns false and len() is bytes-not-elements. This is a misleading clippy::len_without_is_empty workaround: a caller treating is_empty() as 'has no sub-conditions' gets a permanently-false answer. The pair is not referenced anywhere in the workspace (verified by grep).
findings.recommendation: Drop the len()/is_empty() pair entirely (size() is the real API and is used), or rename to byte_size(). If clippy's len_without_is_empty is the concern, prefer removing len() rather than adding a semantically-wrong is_empty().

findings.detail: HEADER_PREFETCH_COUNT (a header-batch count of 2000) is defined inside the inventory-payload module, which is the wrong domain (headers vs inventory hashes), and a grep shows it is unused anywhere in the workspace. Additionally the doc comment on MAX_HASHES_COUNT says 'increased for faster sync' immediately above the value 500 that actually matches C# (InvPayload.MaxHashesCount=500) — a stale, self-contradicting comment from a prior change.
findings.recommendation: Delete the dead HEADER_PREFETCH_COUNT constant (or move it to the headers payload module where it belongs), and remove the stale 'increased for faster sync' line so the comment just states the C# parity value.

findings.detail: MessageFlags::from_byte emits a tracing::warn! for every message whose flag byte has any bit outside 0x01 set. A malicious or buggy peer can set high bits on every frame, producing one WARN log line per inbound message — an unbounded log-amplification / noise vector on the hot P2P receive path. The byte-preserving behaviour itself is correct and protocol-locked; only the per-message WARN is the issue.
findings.recommendation: Needs differential test to confirm byte round-trip is unchanged, then downgrade the per-message log to trace! or rate-limit/aggregate it (e.g. debug-once per peer). Do not change the from_bits_retain byte semantics. (Fix lives in neo-primitives macro, but the impact is realized in neo-p2p's network path.)

findings.detail: MessageCommand::from_byte returns Result<Self, NetworkError> but is now infallible — the macro arm returns Ok(Self::from_byte_unchecked(byte)) unconditionally, always producing Unknown(byte) for unrecognized commands. The parse_error closure (with its placeholder peer 0.0.0.0:0) is only reachable via parse_str (textual form), never the wire byte path. Callers must still unwrap/`?` a Result that can never be Err, and the dummy SocketAddr in the closure is dead on the byte path.
findings.recommendation: Either document that from_byte is infallible-by-design (the Result is for trait-shape symmetry), or expose an infallible const from_byte and reserve Result for parse_str only. At minimum add a doc note so callers do not assume the Err arm is reachable from wire bytes.

findings.detail: C# VersionPayload exposes a computed AllowCompression field (true unless a DisableCompressionCapability is present; VersionPayload.cs Deserialize). The Rust VersionPayload drops it. It is NOT a wire field (C# Size/Serialize omit it), so this is not a serialization bug, but any downstream consumer that needs to honour a peer's compression preference must re-scan `capabilities` for DisableCompression itself, and that derivation is not provided here.
findings.recommendation: Add an inherent helper `allows_compression(&self) -> bool` that returns `!self.capabilities.iter().any(|c| matches!(c, NodeCapability::DisableCompression))`, mirroring C#'s computed property, so consumers do not re-implement it ad hoc.

per_crate.overall: neo-io is a clean, well-documented, security-conscious IO/serialization/caching foundation crate that closely tracks the C# Neo.IO reference. The protocol-critical paths (var-int codec, MemoryReader bounds checking, LZ4 decompress, array deserialization) are carefully DoS-hardened: lengths are bounded before allocation, position arithmetic uses saturating ops, and there are zero panics/unwraps/expects outside test modules. The lib.rs lint directives, layered error type, and reuse of mature crates (lru, bytes, lz4_flex, parking_lot) all conform to CONVENTIONS.md. The issues found are quality/maintenance items, not correctness or consensus bugs: a duplicated writer-extension trait whose two main methods are shadowed-dead, a speculative mutable-capacity API on HashSetCache with no C# analogue and no callers, a stale doc reference to a non-existent module, a fully-dead macro, and a couple of minor redundancies. No findings touch protocol-locked byte behavior.
strengths: DoS-resistant deserialization: deserialize_array/deserialize_array_with/deserialize_exact_array bound count via read_var_int(max) AND re-check count > max before Vec::with_capacity (helper.rs:79-90, 102-113, 125-134); read_nullable_array checks count > max before allocating (extensions/memory_reader.rs:25-32).

decompress_lz4 (compression.rs:30-52) checks the attacker-declared size against max_size BEFORE decoding, then re-validates decompressed length and exact-size match — correct compression-bomb defense with a regression test.

MemoryReader bounds checking is sound: ensure_available uses saturating_add (memory_reader.rs:101), set_position guards position <= len, and read_var_int never indexes out of range; ReadFixedString trailing-zero validation faithfully mirrors C# MemoryReader.cs:198-212.

Zero panic!/unwrap()/expect() in non-test code across the whole crate; HashSetCache gracefully downgrades zero capacity instead of panicking (unlike C# which throws), with a tracing/eprintln fallback.

FIFO cache semantics are correctly preserved on top of the lru crate: reads use peek()/contains() (no reordering) and inserts use insert_if_absent (no value refresh), matching C# FIFOCache.OnAccess being a no-op (cache_entries.rs:74-119).

Idiomatic Rust throughout: const fn where possible, #[must_use], zero-copy read_bytes_ref/as_bytes, iterator combinators (map_or/is_some_and/get_or_insert_with), and the read_buf_primitives! macro removes per-type boilerplate cleanly.

findings.detail: The BinaryWriterExtensions trait declares write_serializable<T> and write_var_string, but BinaryWriter already has identical inherent methods (binary_writer.rs:168 write_serializable, :142 write_var_string). Inherent methods always win name resolution, so these two trait methods are dead unless called via fully-qualified syntax. A workspace grep shows BinaryWriterExtensions is never imported outside neo-io, and the genuinely-unique methods (write_serializable_collection, write_nullable_array, write_fixed_string) have zero external callers either.
findings.recommendation: Drop write_serializable and write_var_string from the trait (they are covered by inherent methods). If the remaining three methods are truly unused, consider whether the trait is needed at all, or fold write_fixed_string/write_nullable_array into inherent methods on BinaryWriter for a single discoverable surface.

findings.detail: HashSetCache exposes set_capacity() plus ensure_backing_capacity()/resize() machinery to support raising capacity at runtime. The C# reference (HashSetCache.cs:32-54) treats _capacity as readonly with no setter, and a workspace grep finds no external caller of set_capacity. try_add() also calls trim_to_capacity() on every insert (including no-op duplicate inserts) which re-walks/re-resizes the backing LruCache, adding work the C# TryAdd (single TryAdd + one RemoveFirst) never does.
findings.recommendation: Make capacity fixed at construction to match C# (drop set_capacity and ensure_backing_capacity). Then try_add can mirror C#: TryAdd the item, and pop_lru once if len > capacity — removing the per-call trim loop.

findings.detail: The IoCache doc comment states 'LRU behavior is implemented by [`crate::caching::lru_cache::LRUCache`] using the upstream lru crate.' There is no lru_cache module or LRUCache type in this crate (mod.rs declares only cache, cache_entries, fifo_cache, hashset_cache, relay_cache). The link is a stale/broken intra-doc reference and the claim is misleading — IoCache is FIFO-only.
findings.recommendation: Remove the broken reference. Reword to describe what actually exists: a thread-safe FIFO cache backed by the lru crate (used in peek-only mode so access order == insertion order).

findings.detail: The impl_from_bytes! macro is defined and documented but has zero invocations anywhere in the workspace (grep finds only the two doc-comment example lines). It is dead surface area in a crate whose macros module is #[macro_use]-exported workspace-wide.
findings.recommendation: Delete impl_from_bytes! (and its doc examples). If a future need arises it can be re-added; per YAGNI it should not ship unused.

findings.detail: write_fixed_string performs two equivalent length checks — value.len() > length (line 48) and bytes.len() > length (line 57) — where bytes = value.as_bytes() so value.len() == bytes.len(); the second branch is unreachable. Padding is written via `&vec![0u8; length - bytes.len()]` which heap-allocates a temporary Vec just to emit zero bytes.
findings.recommendation: Keep a single length check, then pad without allocating, e.g. self.buffer.resize(self.buffer.len() + pad, 0) via a helper, or loop write_u8(0). Removes one redundant check and one allocation per call.

per_crate.overall: neo-node is a small (~2270 LoC), well-organized composition root: main.rs (feature-gated stub vs. daemon), node.rs (CLI/config/storage/P2P/RPC wiring), and consensus.rs (dBFT driver). It is unusually well-documented for an integration crate, follows the layered error strategy (anyhow at the daemon boundary, ? propagation, no internal panics on the protocol path), and has strong, behaviour-level test coverage (TOML parsing/aliases, durable-tip restart resume, RPC smoke test, commit-to-store flush). The shared-DataCache snapshot semantics — which look suspicious at first (consensus reads a "startup_snapshot" that never re-binds) — are actually correct because DataCache::clone shares the same Arc<RwLock<InnerState>>, so validator-set reads stay live. The most impactful issue is dependency bloat: roughly a dozen declared dependencies (incl. mimalloc, prometheus, sysinfo, hyper, chrono, rand, url, zeroize, num_cpus, tracing-appender, async-trait, plus four optional neo-* crates) are never referenced in the crate, which both bloats the build and signals lost intent (most notably mimalloc, which a node binary normally installs as #[global_allocator] but here is dead weight). Remaining findings are lower-severity: a couple of silent ledger-read error swallows (unwrap_or(0)) and minor clarity nits. No correctness or consensus-parity defects found in the wiring itself.
strengths: dBFT extensible<->ConsensusPayload codec authenticates the inbound sender (consensus.rs:107-110: in-body validator_index must map to the validator whose script_hash equals the extensible sender), rejecting spoofed payloads — do not weaken this.

Validator set is sorted by public key before indexing (consensus.rs:137-141) with a dedicated regression test (validators_are_sorted_by_pubkey) — consensus-critical for primary selection / NextConsensus parity with C# OrderBy(p => p).
