Durable-tip restart logic is correct and well-tested: durable_tip is read before the snapshot is moved into contexts, used to seed both ledger_ctx.record_tip and network.set_block_height, with an end-to-end test (build_node_restarts_from_durable_rocksdb_tip_and_resumes_sync_cursor) asserting the sync cursor resumes at tip+1.

Per-block durability via SystemContext::commit_to_store flushing the shared StoreCache (node.rs:299-303) with a dedicated test (commit_to_store_flushes_snapshot_writes_to_durable_store) — this is the fix that prevents the in-memory-only / stuck-at-genesis class of bug; keep it intact.

Config surface is robust: every section is #[serde(default)], C#-style and legacy aliases are accepted, unknown keys/sections are tolerated, and max_connections handles the C# -1=unlimited convention with explicit validation (node.rs:171-182).
Shared-snapshot design (DataCache clones sharing one Arc<RwLock<InnerState>>) is used correctly so the consensus driver observes committed validator-set state without re-reading the store.

findings.location: neo-node/Cargo.toml:39-104 (dependencies); verified zero references in src/
findings.detail: About a dozen declared dependencies are never referenced anywhere in the crate (src/ or tests/): non-optional deps mimalloc, num_cpus, sysinfo, chrono, rand, url, zeroize, prometheus, hyper, tracing-appender, async-trait; and optional crates neo-oracle-service, neo-wallets, neo-manifest, neo-state-service (none of which are enabled by any feature, so they are unreachable). `cargo tree -p neo-node --edges normal` confirms mimalloc/prometheus/sysinfo/num_cpus/chrono are direct normal deps, and grep over src+tests finds zero uses. These bloat compile time and the dependency-audit surface, and are almost certainly residue from the pre-consolidation NeoSystem architecture.
findings.recommendation: Run cargo-machete / cargo-udeps and drop the genuinely unused deps. Decide explicitly on the now-dead optional neo-* crates (remove or wire a feature). Treat mimalloc separately — see the node-best-practice finding.

findings.detail: mimalloc is a direct dependency (with default-features=false, which also disables its global-allocator override feature) but there is no `#[global_allocator] static GLOBAL: MiMalloc = MiMalloc;` anywhere in the binary (grep for global_allocator across neo-node and neo-system returns nothing). A blockchain node binary normally depends on mimalloc precisely to install it as the global allocator for allocation-heavy block/VM workloads; here the intent was either lost in refactoring or the dep is vestigial. As written the node uses the system allocator and pays for the mimalloc build with zero benefit.
findings.recommendation: Either wire mimalloc as the global allocator in main.rs (`#[global_allocator] static GLOBAL: mimalloc::MiMalloc = mimalloc::MiMalloc;`, enabling the appropriate mimalloc feature) if the performance intent is real, or remove the dependency. Do not leave it half-configured.

findings.detail: SystemContext::current_height maps any LedgerContract::current_index() error to 0 via unwrap_or(0), and ledger_tip does the same. Post-genesis the current-index key always exists, so an Err here means storage corruption / a read fault — but it is silently reported as height 0. On the RPC and consensus paths this could make the node behave as if at genesis (mis-advertise height, attempt to re-sync, or mis-derive the next round) instead of surfacing the fault. This is low-probability but is silent error-swallowing on the protocol path.
findings.recommendation: Log a warn! when current_index() returns Err before defaulting to 0 (the genesis/None case can stay quiet), so a storage fault is observable rather than masquerading as a genesis chain.

findings.detail: The doc comment on current_prev_hash (consensus.rs:277-280) says 'the stored snapshot does not advance, so the tip is tracked from start/Imported rather than re-read', which reads as if the snapshot is frozen — but configure_round() DOES re-read validator set and NextConsensus live from that same snapshot (which shares state and does advance). The comment understates that only prev_hash is tracked manually while validator-set state is read live; a future maintainer could wrongly 'fix' this by re-binding the snapshot.
findings.recommendation: Tighten the comment to state that the DataCache shares committed state (so validator-set reads are live) and only prev_hash is tracked locally because it is needed before the Imported event lands.

findings.detail: Every BlockSource method constructs a fresh LedgerContract via neo_native_contracts::LedgerContract::new() inline (8 call sites). LedgerContract is a unit struct so this is zero-cost, but the repetition is noise and obscures that all methods operate over one ledger view. A single associated helper (e.g. `fn ledger() -> LedgerContract`) or a stored field would read better.
findings.recommendation: Store the unit LedgerContract once (field or a private `fn ledger()`), or use LedgerContract::default() consistently, to remove the repeated inline construction. Cosmetic only.

findings.detail: On Ctrl-C the daemon only calls handle.abort() on the spawned tasks; unlike the tests (which call network.shutdown() and server.stop_rpc_server()), the production path does not gracefully stop the P2P listener or the RPC server, nor explicitly drop the RPC keepalive before aborting. Durability is fine because commit_to_store runs per block, but in-flight peers/connections are dropped abruptly. Additionally the inventory forwarder (node.rs:791,837) does `let _ = blockchain.tell(...)` and will spin silently forwarding into a closed command loop if the blockchain service dies, rather than breaking.
findings.recommendation: Add a brief graceful shutdown (network.shutdown(), stop the RPC server) before aborting handles; and in the inventory forwarder, break the loop / log when blockchain.tell returns Err (closed loop) instead of looping forever. Both are operational robustness, not correctness.

per_crate.overall: A well-organized, one-type-per-file crate that closely tracks the C# Neo.Ledger.MemoryPool semantics, with clear C#-citing doc comments and a strong test suite covering admission, conflict eviction, capacity-self-eviction event ordering, and per-failure VerifyResult classification. The hot path (try_add) holds a single write lock across containment + conflict + verify + admit, matching C#'s _txRwLock and correctly serializing sender-fee accounting. The crypto/witness verification (PROTOCOL-LOCKED) faithfully mirrors C# Transaction.Verify including the multisig early-exit loop, GetScriptHashesForVerifying, the v3.10.0 Expired/NotYetValid split, and the conflict-fee rebate. The most impactful issues are not in the byte-level protocol but in pool resource management and dead API: capacity is enforced only over the verified queue (not total pool, diverging from C#), and the reverify/commit_block re-promotion machinery plus the entire TransactionRouter are dead code, leaving transactions parked in the unverified queue after a block persists with no path back to verified. clippy --all-targets is clean.
strengths: try_add correctly holds one write lock across containment, CheckConflicts, verification, and admission (memory_pool.rs:473-569), matching C#'s _txRwLock so concurrent submissions cannot race the same pooled sender-fee state.

CheckConflicts (memory_pool.rs:96-144) faithfully ports C# MemoryPool.cs: only the verified queue is consulted (C# _unsortedTransactions == VerifiedCount), the two-direction conflict scan, the shared-signer disjoint check, and the conflictsFeeSum >= NetworkFee rejection are all correct.

The conflict-fee rebate (memory_pool.rs:497-508) and pooled sender-fee/oracle-duplicate bookkeeping (context_add/context_remove) match C# TransactionVerificationContext.CheckTransaction exactly, including system+network fee summation.

verify_state_dependent (verification.rs:264-392) is an accurate port: validity-window v3.10.0 Expired/NotYetValid split, blocked accounts, ContainsConflictHash gate, balance check, per-attribute HF gating + fees, fee-per-byte coverage, and the signature/multisig fast-path execFeeFactor charging in the documented order.

The capacity-self-eviction test (memory_pool.rs:924-993) pins the subtle C# behavior that TransactionAdded fires before TransactionRemoved and the just-added tx can be the one evicted, returning OutOfMemory.

Clean module layout (one public type per file), consistent doc comments citing concrete C# file:line anchors, and #![deny(unsafe_code)]/#![warn(missing_docs)] per CONVENTIONS.md; clippy --all-targets is warning-free.

findings.detail: Capacity is enforced only against the verified queue: `if guard.verified.len() > guard.capacity` and only verified items are evicted. C# enforces capacity over the TOTAL pool — RemoveOverCapacity loops `do {...} while (Count > Capacity)` where `Count = _unsortedTransactions.Count + _unverifiedTransactions.Count` (MemoryPool.cs:106, :433), and GetLowestFeeTransaction (MemoryPool.cs:269-283) evicts the global lowest-fee item, preferring the unverified pool when its min is <= the verified min. Because try_add admits straight into verified and update_pool_for_block_persisted dumps survivors into unverified with no capacity check, the pool can hold up to `capacity` verified PLUS up to `capacity` unverified simultaneously — roughly double the configured memory_pool_max_transactions. This weakens the DoS bound the capacity setting exists to enforce.
findings.recommendation: Track total occupancy (verified.len()+unverified.len()) for the capacity gate, and evict the global lowest-fee item (preferring the unverified queue when its min <= verified min) in a loop until total <= capacity, mirroring C# GetLowestFeeTransaction/RemoveOverCapacity. Add a differential test against C# behavior since this affects resource bounding.

findings.detail: `reverify` and `commit_block` are public but have zero callers outside this crate's own tests (the only block-persist caller in neo-blockchain/src/service.rs:283,307 uses update_pool_for_block_persisted). update_pool_for_block_persisted (step 3, lines 359-369) moves every surviving verified transaction into the unverified queue and clears all verified-only bookkeeping, mirroring C# InvalidateVerifiedTransactions — but C# then re-promotes them via a background ReverifyTransactions pass. Here nothing ever calls reverify, so after the first persisted block the verified queue is repopulated only by fresh try_add admissions; previously-pooled txs are stranded in unverified and served only via unverified_snapshot(). This is a functional gap (verified-queue starvation) rather than a divergence in this file alone.
findings.recommendation: Either wire a periodic reverify pass from the blockchain/consensus driver (the intended consumer) or document the contract that the consumer MUST drive reverify after update_pool_for_block_persisted. If no consumer will ever drive it, remove the dead reverify/commit_block API to avoid implying functionality that does not run.

findings.detail: `TransactionVerificationContext` (confirmed/historic sets + confirm/rotate/all/contains) is misnamed and effectively dead. The real C# TransactionVerificationContext semantics (_senderFee, _oracleResponses) are implemented in the separate sender_fees/oracle_responses maps on MemoryPoolInner. The struct's only live use is `verification_context.confirm(hash)` (lines 298, 385) writing into a HashSet that is never read by any pool logic, plus full reset at line 367. `rotate()`, `all()`, `historic`, and `contains()` have no callers anywhere in the workspace. This adds a confusingly-named no-op abstraction on the consensus-critical persist path.
findings.recommendation: Remove the verification_context field and the confirm() calls (they accomplish nothing), or delete the module entirely. If the C# name should be preserved, rename the struct to reflect what it actually does and fold the sender_fees/oracle_responses maps into it so it matches the C# class it claims to mirror.

findings.detail: `TransactionRouter` is never instantiated or called anywhere (grep across the workspace finds zero `.preverify(` calls and zero `TransactionRouter::new` outside this file's tests). Only the `PreverifyCompleted` struct is reused by neo-blockchain, and it is constructed directly there (handlers.rs:931,946), not via the router. Moreover `preverify` runs `Verifiable::verify` (state-independent), which try_add already performs in full via verify_transaction, so even if used it would be redundant work on the admission path.
findings.recommendation: Keep the `PreverifyCompleted` struct (it is used cross-crate) but delete the unused `TransactionRouter`, or document why the router exists if a future consumer is planned. Avoid carrying a parallel verification entry point that duplicates try_add's state-independent pass.

findings.detail: PoolIndex exposes `items: BTreeSet` and `hashes: HashMap` as public fields, and memory_pool.rs mutates them directly: `guard.verified.items.clear(); guard.verified.hashes.clear();` (lines 364-365) and `guard.verified.items.iter().next()` to find the lowest item (line 553). The two fields must stay in sync to preserve the invariant that hashes mirrors items; manual dual-mutation invites desync and bypasses the insert/remove methods that keep them consistent.
findings.recommendation: Make items/hashes private and add `clear(&mut self)` and a `lowest()`/`pop_lowest()` method to PoolIndex; have memory_pool.rs call those instead of touching the fields, so the index invariant is enforced in one place.

findings.detail: `gas_balance_of` hand-rolls the NEP-17 AccountState decode (build key with prefix 20, BinarySerializer::deserialize, expect StackItem::Struct, take field 0 as_int, default to zero). The identical decode already exists privately as read_gas_account in neo-native-contracts/src/gas_token.rs:37-54 (the natural owner) and again in neo-rpc/src/server/wallet_compat.rs:350. Three independent copies of the same consensus-relevant balance decode risk drifting apart.
findings.recommendation: Expose a single public reader from neo-native-contracts (e.g. GasToken::balance_of(snapshot, account) -> BigInt) and have neo-mempool and neo-rpc call it, removing the hand-rolled copies.

per_crate.overall: The MPT-storage core (mpt_store.rs) is the strongest, most carefully-engineered part of this crate: the copy-on-write generation snapshot design for reader isolation is correct, well-documented, and pinned against a real C# 3.9.2 reference vector (roots + proof node set), with a concurrency stress test. Wire formats (StateRoot unsigned bytes, Vote var-bytes max-64, Keys BE/LE split, MessageType) are byte-faithful to the vendored C# 3.9.1 sources. The headline problem is not bugs in what runs but a large dead/unwired surface: an entire parallel "verification pipeline" — StateStore candidate/validated cache, Verifier, StateServiceCommitHandlers (whose CommittingHandler is an explicit no-op stub), SyntheticStateRootCalculator, StateRootCache, and the metrics counters — exists with zero production callers. The RPC handlers that DO ship (getstateroot/getstateheight) read the never-populated in-memory StateStore rather than the populated MptStore, so they are effectively non-functional in production. The crate would shrink substantially and become clearer if the unwired half were either wired or deleted.
strengths: mpt_store.rs reader-snapshot isolation (Arc<BTreeMap> COW generation map + write_gate, make_mut publish) is a correct, well-documented solution to the prune-out-from-under-a-reader hazard; backed by roots_and_proof_match_csharp_reference_vector (real C# 3.9.2 pinned roots + proof node set) and concurrent_apply_and_snapshot_reads_stay_consistent (50-block/4-reader stress).

Wire/byte parity is solid and must not be disturbed: StateRoot::unsigned_bytes (version u8 + index u32 LE + 32-byte hash), the encode_state_root trailing var-int-0 witness, Keys::state_root big-endian index key vs CURRENT_LOCAL_ROOT_INDEX little-endian value (kv_layout_matches_csharp_key_scheme pins both against C# Keys.cs/BitConverter), and Vote's var_bytes{max:64} matching C# ReadVarMemory(64).
Vote deserialization is DoS-bounded via impl_serializable var_bytes{max:64}; untrusted P2P signature length cannot trigger an unbounded allocation.
MessageType uses the shared protocol_enum! macro with full from_byte/to_byte/serde round-trip tests including the out-of-range (2 => None) rejection.

StateRootCache correctly uses the lru crate with NonZeroUsize and Relaxed atomic stats counters; apply_block_changes ignores trie.delete's return value with a comment explaining the C# no-op-on-absent-key semantics.

findings.detail: An entire 'verification pipeline' is public API but has zero production callers (confirmed by grep across the workspace: try_add_state_root, commit_validated_state_roots, discard, Verifier::new, StateServiceCommitHandlers, SyntheticStateRootCalculator, StateRootCache, record_ingest_result are referenced only from this crate's own tests). StateServiceCommitHandlers::blockchain_committing_handler (commit_handlers.rs:109-120) is an explicit no-op with a 'Real implementation would compute the state root here' comment, and blockchain_committed_handler (commit_handlers.rs:101-107) just discards block.index(). This is speculative/dead flexibility: two parallel state-root subsystems (the live MptStore and the dormant verification cache) where only one is wired.
findings.recommendation: Decide which path is canonical. Either wire StateServiceCommitHandlers into the block-persist pipeline and have it drive MptStore::apply_block_changes (the real MPT, not SyntheticStateRootCalculator), or delete the verification-cache half (StateStore candidate machinery, Verifier, commit_handlers, SyntheticStateRootCalculator, StateRootCache, metrics) until a consumer exists. Do not keep both indefinitely.

findings.detail: getstateheight reads StateStore::current_local_index() and getstateroot reads StateStore::get_state_root(ByBlockIndex) from the in-memory verification cache (by_index/by_root_hash), but nothing in production ever calls try_add_state_root, so that cache is always empty. The populated state-root records live in MptStore (apply_block_changes writes Keys::state_root + CURRENT_LOCAL_ROOT_INDEX, exposed via MptStore::get_state_root / current_local_root_index) and are NOT consulted by these two handlers. Net effect: a running node serves getstateheight as null and getstateroot as 'unknown state root' even after blocks are applied.
findings.recommendation: Have getstateheight/getstateroot fall back to (or read primarily from) MptStore::current_local_root_index / MptStore::get_state_root when the StateStore has an MPT backend, so the served height/root reflect the trie that apply_block_changes actually maintains. Add an integration test that applies a block and asserts getstateroot returns it.

findings.detail: The doc on cached_hash (line 26) and on hash() (line 63) say 'double-SHA256', but hash() computes a single Crypto::sha256 over unsigned_bytes (state_root.rs:65-73). The single hash is actually CORRECT for C# parity — C# StateRoot.Hash => CalculateHash => ms.ToArray().Sha256() and the C# Sha256() extension is SHA256.HashData (single, per neo_csharp Cryptography/Helper.cs:113). So the code is right and the comment is wrong; a future reader 'fixing' the code to match the comment would break byte parity.
findings.recommendation: Change both doc comments from 'double-SHA256' to 'single SHA256 (matches C# IVerifiable.CalculateHash, which Sha256()-hashes the unsigned bytes once)'. Leave the code as-is.

findings.detail: commit_validated_state_roots only removes hashes from the candidate set; the value already lives in by_index/by_root_hash from try_add_state_root, so 'commit' performs no validation transition beyond candidate removal (the body is mostly a comment explaining it does nothing). Combined with StateStoreTransaction::commit (line 79) calling it on self.store, which begin_transaction (line 126-132) built as an independent StateStore::clone of the source store — so a transaction 'commit' mutates a throwaway copy and is invisible to the original store. The transactional abstraction therefore does not actually commit anything to the shared store.
findings.recommendation: Either make StateStoreTransaction hold an Arc<StateStore> to the real store (so commit is observable) or remove the transaction type entirely (it has no production caller). Clarify that commit_validated_state_roots is purely a candidate-set demotion, or drop the candidate concept if no witness-verification pipeline will consume it.

findings.detail: on_reverting iterates from_index..=to_index and discards each candidate, but get_state_root(ByBlockIndex) returns at most one root per index and the reverting handler has no production caller. More notably it relies on by_index holding exactly the candidate to revert; because try_add_state_root overwrites by_index on index collision (state_store.rs:143 inserts unconditionally) but rejects on root-hash collision (line 138), an index that received two distinct-hash candidates keeps only the last in by_index while both remain in by_root_hash — so a revert leaves an orphaned by_root_hash entry. This is latent given the dead code, but is a real inconsistency if the pipeline is ever wired.
findings.recommendation: If the candidate pipeline is kept, make try_add_state_root reject (or explicitly replace and clean up) on duplicate block index, and have discard/on_reverting reconcile both maps. Otherwise this disappears with the dead-code removal above.

findings.detail: StateStoreInner has a hand-written Clone impl that just clones each field; all three fields (BTreeMap, HashMap, HashSet) are Clone, so this is a needless manual impl. (The StateStore Clone at lines 187-198 is legitimate because RwLock is not Clone.)
findings.recommendation: Replace the manual impl with #[derive(Clone)] on StateStoreInner (it already derives Debug, Default).

findings.detail: metrics.rs hand-rolls two process-global AtomicU64 counters (ACCEPTED/REJECTED) with record_ingest_result/state_root_ingest_stats, duplicating the more capable StateRootCacheStats already in root_cache.rs (which tracks hits/misses/insertions/evictions with a snapshot type). Both counter systems are unused in production. Global mutable counters also make the values un-resettable between test runs and non-instance-scoped.
findings.recommendation: If observability is needed later, expose it through the existing per-instance StateRootCacheStats (or the workspace metrics facade) rather than a second global counter module; delete metrics.rs if the ingest pipeline stays unwired.

per_crate.overall: neo-hsm is a small, well-structured crate (~1729 LoC incl. tests) providing a unified async `HsmSigner` trait with Simulation, PKCS#11, and Ledger backends behind feature flags. It follows the workspace conventions well: `#![deny(unsafe_code)]`/`#![warn(missing_docs)]`, crate-internal `thiserror` error type (`HsmError`/`HsmResult`), workspace-pinned internal dep on neo-crypto, `zeroize` for in-memory private keys, and `OsRng` (not thread_rng) for key generation. The Neo-specific crypto plumbing (signature redeem script, script-hash-from-pubkey, Base58Check address) is correct and pinned by good unit tests. The most impactful issues are: (1) the public crate-level doc example references a `HsmRuntime` type that does not exist, so the documented entry point is wrong; (2) the Ledger `send_apdu` does single-frame HID I/O only with brittle length handling on untrusted device bytes; (3) the PKCS#11 signing path does not normalize ECDSA s to low-s, a C#-parity / malleability concern that needs a differential test; and (4) `der_to_raw` silently truncates oversized r/s components instead of erroring. There is also modest duplication between the PKCS#11 key-finding helpers and the repeated HsmKeyInfo-with-label construction. None of these are consensus-path bugs since this crate is operator-facing key management, but the signing-format concerns matter for produced signatures.
strengths: Layered error strategy is followed correctly: crate-internal thiserror `HsmError` with `HsmResult` alias and a clean `From<anyhow::Error>` bridge; no `anyhow` leaking across the public API (error.rs:1-96).

Security-conscious defaults: private keys held in `Zeroizing<[u8;32]>` (sim_signer.rs:24-27), key generation uses `OsRng` with an explicit SECURITY comment warning against thread_rng (sim_signer.rs:56-59).

Neo crypto plumbing is correct and pinned by tests: `signature_redeem_script`/`script_hash_from_public_key` build the PUSHDATA1+SYSCALL CheckSig script and hash160 it, validated against a known script hash and asserted to differ from a naive hash160(pubkey) (hsm_signer.rs:109-178).

`der_to_raw` carefully bounds-checks every index of the untrusted Ledger DER response and returns an error rather than panicking, with a clear doc comment explaining the threat model (ledger_signer.rs:305-369).

Consistent SHA-256 signing contract documented on the trait and honored across backends: the trait doc mandates SHA-256(data) (hsm_signer.rs:88-92); PKCS#11 explicitly pre-hashes with `Crypto::sha256` for raw `Mechanism::Ecdsa` (pkcs11_signer.rs:396), and the simulation path relies on neo-crypto's internal SHA-256 — keeping outputs equivalent.

Clean feature gating and re-export hygiene in lib.rs; backend modules are cfg-gated both at `mod` and `pub use` so a default build only compiles simulation (lib.rs:40-69, pkcs11/mod.rs, ledger/mod.rs).

findings.detail: The crate-level usage doc example imports and calls `HsmRuntime` (`use neo_hsm::{HsmConfig, HsmRuntime, HsmDeviceType};` and `HsmRuntime::new(config).await?`), but no `HsmRuntime` type exists anywhere in the crate (grep confirms the only matches are these doc lines). The public entry point the docs advertise is fictional, so a user following the documentation cannot compile it. The example is marked `ignore`, which is why this was never caught by doctests. Either the runtime type was removed during consolidation and the docs not updated, or the example was aspirational.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the example to use the types that actually exist (e.g. construct a `SimulationSigner`/`Pkcs11Signer`/`LedgerSigner` and call the `HsmSigner` trait methods `unlock`/`sign`), and drop `ignore` so the example is compile-checked. If a `HsmRuntime` facade is intended, that is a separate feature, not a doc fix.

findings.detail: `send_apdu` does a single `write` + single `read_timeout` of one 64-byte HID frame and then parses the response from that one frame. Ledger's APDU-over-HID transport (HID framing tag 0x05) splits responses larger than ~57 payload bytes across multiple sequenced frames; this code never reads continuation frames. For GetPublicKey (~33-byte pubkey + address) it likely fits, but a SignTransaction response (DER signature) or any larger reply can be silently truncated. Worse, `data_len` is taken from the device-reported length bytes and then clamped with `data_len.min(response.len() - 7)` (line 141), so a device claiming more bytes than were read yields a short `data` slice that then flows into status-word parsing and `der_to_raw`. This is untrusted-input parsing on the operator key path; truncation produces a wrong/garbage signature rather than a clear error.
findings.recommendation: Implement proper Ledger HID frame reassembly: read the first frame to get the total APDU length, then loop reading continuation frames (incrementing sequence) until the full payload is collected; reject if the reported length is not fully received. Treat a short read as an explicit `DeviceError`, never a clamp-and-continue. This is hardware-transport code, not protocol bytes, so it can be refactored (no differential-test gate needed), but it must be exercised against a real device or a recorded fixture.

findings.detail: The PKCS#11 sign path returns whatever the token produces for `Mechanism::Ecdsa` over SHA-256(data) without normalizing the signature's s value to the low half-order. Neo/C# and the rest of this codebase normalize secp256r1/secp256k1 signatures (neo-crypto normalizes s for k256; the p256 `sign` used by the simulation path emits low-s by default). A PKCS#11 token is free to emit high-s, which would be a valid ECDSA signature but may fail Neo verification or diverge from C# parity expectations for the same key/message. There is no normalization and no test covering this, so a real HSM could produce signatures the network rejects.
findings.recommendation: After obtaining the raw r||s from the token, normalize s to low-s (s = n - s when s > n/2) before returning, matching the rest of neo-crypto. Because this affects produced signature bytes on the protocol path, add a differential test against C#/neo-crypto verification rather than only a unit test. Flag as needs-differential-test, not a free refactor.

findings.detail: After parsing r and s from the DER response, the final padding step silently truncates over-length components instead of rejecting them: `raw[r_offset..32].copy_from_slice(&r[r.len().saturating_sub(32)..])` keeps only the low 32 bytes when `r.len() > 32` (the leading-zero strip at line 333 only removes a single 0x00 byte, so a malformed r of length 34 would be truncated rather than erroring). For a well-formed secp256r1 signature r/s are <=33 bytes and this is fine, but on malformed device input it converts a parse error into a wrong-but-well-formed 64-byte signature, defeating the careful bounds-checking above it.
findings.recommendation: After stripping leading zeros, assert `r.len() <= 32 && s.len() <= 32` and return the `invalid()` error otherwise, instead of `.min(32)`/`saturating_sub(32)` truncation.

findings.detail: `find_private_key` and `find_public_key` are near-identical: each does a label lookup then a hex-id fallback lookup, differing only in `ObjectClass::PRIVATE_KEY` vs `ObjectClass::PUBLIC_KEY`. The label+id fallback search block is duplicated four times across the two functions. Separately, the 'build HsmKeyInfo then conditionally attach label' pattern is repeated verbatim in sim_signer.rs (list_keys lines 182-188 and get_key lines 198-202) and pkcs11_signer.rs (215-218).
findings.recommendation: Extract one `find_object(session, class, key_id)` helper parameterized by `ObjectClass` (collapses both finders), and add a small `HsmKeyInfo::new(...).with_optional_label(label)` or local helper to remove the repeated label-attach branches.

findings.detail: `set_pin` contains `let _ = &self.device_info; // Silence unused field warning`. This is a code smell hiding a real signal: `device_info` is read by the `device_info()` trait method, so the field is genuinely used — the suppression line is dead/misleading and suggests confusion about why the warning appeared (it would not normally fire here). It clutters a public test helper.
findings.recommendation: Delete the `let _ = &self.device_info;` line; the field is used by the trait impl and needs no suppression. If a warning does appear, address the actual cause rather than masking it.

findings.detail: `find_ledger_device` and `send_apdu` each rebuild the full Ledger device list via `hid_api.device_list().filter(...).collect::<Vec<_>>()` on every call (lines 59-62 and 91-95), an allocation + full enumeration of all HID devices per APDU round-trip. `device.manufacturer_string().unwrap_or("Ledger")` etc. are reasonable, but the repeated collect-then-index pattern could use `.nth(index)` to avoid materializing the whole filtered Vec when only one element is needed.
findings.recommendation: Use `hid_api.device_list().filter(|d| d.vendor_id() == LEDGER_VENDOR_ID).nth(index as usize)` to avoid the intermediate Vec allocation; keep a separate count only for the error message when needed. Minor, since HID enumeration cost dominates anyway.

per_crate.overall: A small, mostly clean configuration crate whose load-bearing surface is `ProtocolSettings` (heavily consumed by neo-rpc, neo-execution, neo-native-contracts via the per-instance `is_hardfork_enabled`). The protocol-critical logic — `ensure_omitted_hardforks`, `validate_hardfork_sequence`, the csharp_default-vs-mainnet loader fallback distinction, and the differential round-trip tests against pinned v3.10.0 JSON — is correct and faithfully mirrors C# `ProtocolSettings`, with good no-panic discipline in constructors. The main issues are not correctness bugs but accumulated dead surface from the consolidation: an entire error module (`ConfigError`/`ConfigResult`) plus three crate dependencies (`uuid`, `dirs`, and effectively `toml`) are unused; a global mutable `HardforkManager` singleton + free function exist but are never called on the protocol path (the per-`ProtocolSettings` method is used instead) and are a determinism footgun; the lib.rs doc example references a `Settings` API that does not exist; and the standby-committee/seed-node literals plus the whole `GenesisConfig` type are duplicated and unused outside the crate. None of these is consensus-breaking, but they obscure the crate's real (small) public contract and add maintenance weight.
strengths: Hardfork sequence validation (protocol.rs:468-500 validate_hardfork_sequence + 384-397 ensure_omitted_hardforks) is a correct port of C# CheckingHardfork/EnsureOmmitedHardforks: gap detection via index distance and monotonic-height checks match neo_csharp/src/Neo/ProtocolSettings.cs:238-281. Do not 'simplify' this; it is consensus-relevant and pinned by tests.

The csharp_default() vs mainnet()/testnet() distinction (protocol.rs:145-166, 282-289) correctly mirrors C#'s subtle loader semantics: omitted ProtocolConfiguration fields fall back to the bare Default record (Network=0, empty committee), NOT to MainNet. The test load_missing_protocol_fields_uses_csharp_default_not_mainnet (protocol.rs:606) pins this; it is an easy thing to get wrong.

Differential round-trip tests (protocol.rs:624-759) deserialize the actual v3.10.0 config.mainnet.json / config.testnet.json shape and assert equality with the built-in presets via PartialEq — exactly the kind of parity test the protocol-locked values need.

No-panic discipline in public constructors: GenesisConfig::private (genesis.rs:240-256) falls back to timestamp 0 instead of unwrapping SystemTime; committee parsing returns typed Result rather than panicking on bad hex (parse_committee, protocol.rs:519-541). The only expect()s are on embedded constant data with clear justification messages.

ProtocolConfigError (protocol.rs:30-73) is a well-structured thiserror enum with #[from] conversions and a From<String>/From<&str> bridge for legacy call sites — good adherence to the crate-internal typed-error convention.

findings.detail: HardforkManager carries a global mutable singleton (static INSTANCE: LazyLock<RwLock<HardforkManager>>), an instance() accessor, a register() mutator, and a free is_hardfork_enabled() that reads the global. A workspace grep shows NONE of these are called outside neo-config (only its own tests exercise them). Every protocol/consensus call site uses the per-instance ProtocolSettings::is_hardfork_enabled (e.g. neo-mempool/src/verification.rs:89, neo-native-contracts/src/policy_contract.rs:213, neo-execution/src/application_engine/state.rs:18). A process-global, runtime-mutable hardfork table is a determinism footgun on a blockchain node (any code could register() and shift activation heights for everyone), and it is dead besides.
findings.recommendation: Delete the global INSTANCE, instance(), the free is_hardfork_enabled(), and likely register()/Default-via-new machinery. Keep mainnet()/testnet()/all()/get_hardforks() which feed the ProtocolSettings presets. Drop the now-unused is_hardfork_enabled re-export from lib.rs:34.

findings.location: neo-config/Cargo.toml:18-25 (uuid, dirs, toml) and neo-config/src/error.rs (whole file)
findings.detail: Three declared dependencies are unused: `uuid` and `dirs` have zero references in src/ (grep confirms), and `toml` is referenced only by ConfigError::TomlError/TomlSerError variants that are never constructed (the loader parses JSON via serde_json, never TOML). The entire error.rs module (ConfigError/ConfigResult) is unused outside the crate; genesis.rs uses only ConfigError::GenesisError, and ProtocolSettings uses its own ProtocolConfigError. So ConfigError exists mostly to host dead variants that drag in the toml crate.
findings.recommendation: Remove uuid and dirs from Cargo.toml. Either drop the TomlError/TomlSerError/ReadError/FileNotFound variants (and the toml dep) or, simpler, collapse genesis.rs's one error use onto ProtocolConfigError or a tiny local enum and delete error.rs + the toml dep. Verify with `cargo build -p neo-config` (already warning-clean) and a dependency audit.

findings.detail: The crate-level doc example references `Settings::from_file("config.toml")` and `Settings::default_for_network(NetworkType::MainNet)`, but no `Settings` type, `from_file`, or `default_for_network` exists anywhere in the crate (grep confirms). The real loader is `ProtocolSettings::load`/`load_from_stream` and it parses JSON, not TOML. This is the first thing a reader of the public API sees and it is entirely fictional, which undermines trust in the rest of the docs.
findings.recommendation: Rewrite the example to use the real API, e.g. `ProtocolSettings::mainnet()` / `ProtocolSettings::load(path)`, and drop the TOML reference (config is JSON for protocol settings).

findings.detail: The 21-entry MainNet standby-committee pubkey list is hardcoded 3 times in genesis.rs (validators[] + committee[]) and again in protocol.rs::mainnet() (and once more inline in the test JSON); TestNet likewise. Seed-node lists are duplicated between network_type.rs::seed_nodes() and protocol.rs::mainnet()/testnet(). Compounding this, GenesisConfig/GenesisValidator/TokenDistribution and NetworkType are not referenced anywhere outside neo-config (grep confirms), so genesis.rs is duplicated data that nothing consumes — the live source of committee/validator data is ProtocolSettings.standby_committee + validators_count.
findings.recommendation: Either derive GenesisConfig/NetworkType seed data from the ProtocolSettings presets (single source of truth) or, given they are unused externally, consider whether GenesisConfig and NetworkType::seed_nodes should be removed entirely. At minimum factor the committee/seed literals into shared const arrays so MainNet keys live in one place.

findings.detail: ProtocolSettings has a `max_block_size` field that is NOT part of the C# ProtocolSettings record (C# has no MaxBlockSize property — confirmed in neo_csharp/src/Neo/ProtocolSettings.cs). The ProtocolConfiguration deserialization struct has no `MaxBlockSize` field, so a config that specifies it is silently ignored (always defaults to constants::MAX_BLOCK_SIZE). A workspace grep shows `.max_block_size` is never read outside this crate, so the field is dead. The round-trip parity tests pass only because both loaded and preset sides default it identically. This is a known architectural note (MaxBlockSize belongs to dBFT settings, not ProtocolSettings); flagging it here as a dead, non-C# field that creates a silent-ignore trap if anyone later wires it to config.
findings.recommendation: Either remove max_block_size from ProtocolSettings (it diverges from the C# record and nothing consumes it) or, if a future consumer needs it, also add it to ProtocolConfiguration + from_raw so config values are honored and add a differential test. Do not change the byte/limit value itself.

findings.detail: load_from_stream uses stream.read_to_end into an unbounded Vec before parsing. For the intended use (a trusted local config file) this is fine, but the method is public and takes `&mut dyn Read`, so an unbounded read on an arbitrary reader is a mild footgun. Not on the protocol path and not a DoS vector for normal node operation.
findings.recommendation: Low priority: document that the reader must be a bounded/trusted config source, or cap the read (e.g. take(MAX_CONFIG_BYTES)). No behavioral change needed for current callers.

per_crate.overall: neo-system is a small, well-documented service-composition root (reth-style `Node` + `NodeBuilder` + a `TypeId`-keyed `ServiceRegistry` + a `WalletProvider`). The structural code is clean and idiomatic: builder fields are all `Option` with descriptive `build()`-time errors and no `unwrap` in the public path, the registry's two `downcast` `expect`s are provably-infallible (keyed by `TypeId::of::<T>()`), error handling matches CONVENTIONS (crate-internal thiserror `NodeError`), and the public API is thoroughly doc-commented with good tests. The most impactful problems are not in the composition machinery but in two leftover stubs that sit on live paths: `TxRouterHandle::try_enqueue_preverify` silently discards transactions yet is actually called by the oracle service to relay signed responses, and `Node::cancellation_token`'s doc contract directly contradicts its implementation. A secondary concern is the legacy `Node::new` constructor with two dead `Option<()>` parameters that drops its service receivers, used broadly across test code.
strengths: NodeBuilder is exemplary: every field is Option, build() returns descriptive NodeError::missing_config/missing_service instead of panicking, and the module doc explicitly guarantees no Option::unwrap in any public method (builder.rs:1-12, 142-179). Do not regress this into unwrap-based construction.

ServiceRegistry's two downcast expect() calls are genuinely infallible because the map is keyed by TypeId::of::<T>() and only register::<T> inserts under that key — the expect messages document exactly this invariant (service_registry.rs:52-70). This is correct, not a reachable panic.

Error handling follows CONVENTIONS.md: a crate-internal thiserror NodeError with ToString-based constructors, plus a NodeResult alias (error.rs). Lint directives #![deny(unsafe_code)]/#![warn(missing_docs)] are present (lib.rs:61-62).

Clone semantics are correct and clearly documented: Node is cheap to clone (handles + Arc), ServiceRegistry/WalletProvider clones share the same inner Arc<RwLock> so a service registered through one clone is visible through all (service_registry.rs:24-33, with a passing test clones_share_the_same_map).
Test coverage is good for a crate this size: builder required-field tests, registry round-trip/replace/shared-Arc tests, and the legacy re-export smoke test all assert real behavior.

findings.detail: TxRouterHandle::try_enqueue_preverify is a no-op stub that always returns Ok(()) and drops the transaction. It is not dead code: the oracle service builds a fully-signed oracle-response transaction and submits it via self.system.tx_router_actor().try_enqueue_preverify(tx_mut, true), then logs 'oracle response tx relayed' on the Ok — but nothing is relayed. The transaction is silently discarded on a protocol path. The doc comment even asserts 'a real implementation forwards to the mempool / network actor', yet the Node already owns both `mempool: Arc<MemoryPool>` and `network: NetworkHandle`, so the seam exists but is unwired. This makes the oracle subsystem appear functional while producing no on-chain effect.
findings.recommendation: Wire TxRouterHandle to the Node's real mempool + network (admit to mempool, broadcast via NetworkHandle when relay=true), or — if relaying is genuinely not ready — make the stub return Err so the oracle logs the warn! branch instead of a false 'relayed' success. Pair with a differential/integration test that asserts the oracle tx actually reaches the mempool.

findings.detail: cancellation_token() is documented as 'Returns a fresh cancellation token, separated from the node's own so the caller can use it independently' but the body is `self.shutdown.clone()` — tokio_util CancellationToken::clone yields a token that shares cancellation state, so cancelling it cancels the node and vice versa. The contract is the opposite of what is implemented. The accompanying test is even named cancellation_token_clone_is_independent while asserting that cancelling the returned token cancels node.shutdown (i.e. NOT independent). A caller trusting the doc would create what it believes is a scoped token and be surprised the whole node shuts down. The migrate_from_neosystem example's ctrl-c handler relies on the (correct) shared behavior.
findings.recommendation: Fix the doc to state the returned token shares the node's shutdown state (cancelling it shuts the node down), and rename the test to reflect shared-state semantics (e.g. cancellation_token_shares_node_shutdown).

findings.detail: Node::new carries two dead parameters `_blockchain: Option<()>` and `_network: Option<()>` that are ignored, and it internally creates handles via BlockchainHandle::with_capacity() / NetworkHandle::channel(8,8) while dropping the corresponding receivers (_rx, _nrx, _etx). The resulting Node has blockchain/network handles wired to channels with no live consumer — sends will eventually error or fill the buffer. This is a legacy NeoSystem-shaped signature kept only for test convenience, yet it is used in production-adjacent tests across neo-rpc and neo-oracle-service (rpc_blockchain_getrawtransaction_vmstate.rs:139, validate_address.rs:19, tracker_base.rs:452, oracle response_tx/dedup tests). The Option<()> args are pure noise and the constructor duplicates NodeBuilder::build()'s native-contract install + field wiring.
findings.recommendation: Drop the two Option<()> parameters (or replace Node::new with a Node::headless(settings) helper that routes through NodeBuilder to avoid duplicating build()'s wiring and the neo_native_contracts::install() call). Keep the dead-channel behavior documented as headless-only.

findings.detail: peek() uses inner.try_read(), which returns None when a writer holds the lock (e.g. during install()/clear()). The only caller is the Debug impl's has_wallet field, so under write contention Debug reports has_wallet:false even when a wallet is loaded — a misleading-but-not-dangerous diagnostic. Acceptable as a non-blocking Debug, but the false-negative is undocumented.
findings.recommendation: Either document that has_wallet is a best-effort, non-blocking probe (false under contention), or have Debug render an 'unknown (locked)' state when try_read() fails rather than collapsing to None.

findings.detail: back_compat.rs is a pure-documentation module: it carries #![allow(missing_docs)] followed by further //! inner-doc blocks (the migration tables) after the attribute, plus a smoke test that only exercises the re-exports already covered by crate::legacy. It contains no code and the migration tables it documents duplicate information already implied by the crate::legacy re-export list in lib.rs:84-98. The unusual ordering (attribute then more //!) and the doc-only module add maintenance surface (two places listing the same type-rename map) with little runtime value.
findings.recommendation: Consider folding the migration table into the crate::legacy module doc (single source of truth) and either removing back_compat.rs or keeping it as a thin pointer. Low priority — it is harmless documentation.

per_crate.overall: neo-runtime is a small (~770 LoC), exceptionally well-documented crate that defines the reth-style service vocabulary for the node: four async service traits (BlockExecutor, NetworkService, ConsensusService, NeoEngine), a BlockchainEvent broadcast enum, a layered ServiceError type, and placeholder outcome/payload structs. The code itself is clean, idiomatic, and largely free of bugs — there is no untrusted-input handling, no panics on a protocol path, no allocation hot-loops (it is pure type/trait definitions). The error type, channel-capacity constants, and event enums are correct and conventional. The two most impactful issues are documentation/architecture honesty problems rather than logic bugs: (1) four broken intra-doc links to BlockchainHandle/BlockchainCommand types that were moved to neo-blockchain but still documented as living here, and (2) three of the four service traits (BlockExecutor, ConsensusService, NeoEngine) plus their builder wiring slots have zero concrete implementors and zero call-sites anywhere in the workspace — they are speculative Stage-A scaffolding. Three declared dependencies (futures, parking_lot, tracing) are entirely unused. None of these threaten consensus or correctness; they are maintainability and clarity concerns.
strengths: Excellent, accurate module/API documentation throughout (lib.rs re-export index, services.rs reth-trait cheat-sheet, NetworkEvent::PeerConnected doc precisely mirrors C# LocalNode inbound-listener-port upgrade semantics at outcome.rs:128-143).

ServiceError follows the CONVENTIONS.md layered-error strategy correctly: a single narrow boundary vocabulary with category()/is_retryable() helpers and ToString-based constructors, deliberately not leaking foreign error types (errors.rs:16-123). Well covered by tests.
Channel capacities are named constants with rationale (blockchain.rs:17-25) rather than magic numbers, and the broadcast lag-vs-block semantics are documented (services.rs:113-117).
#![deny(unsafe_code)] / #![warn(missing_docs)] lint directives present and the crate genuinely has no unsafe and full doc coverage on public items (lib.rs:83-84).

NetworkService is a real, used abstraction: implemented by neo-network LocalNodeService (local_node.rs:489) and consumed across neo-network/neo-system/neo-rpc — this is the trait worth keeping stable.
Tests are meaningful for the data types (field-wise event equality, error category stability, outcome constructors) and assert trait object-safety explicitly (services.rs:191-197).

findings.detail: Four intra-doc links reference `BlockchainHandle` and `BlockchainCommand` as if they live in this crate (re-export index row, services.rs cheat-sheet, prose). Those types were moved to neo-blockchain (commit d794bf35 dropped the neo-runtime duplicate), so `RUSTDOCFLAGS="-D rustdoc::broken_intra_doc_links" cargo doc -p neo-runtime` fails with 4 `unresolved link` errors. The docs also assert this crate 'owns ... the BlockchainHandle command/event channel' (lib.rs:27-28), which is no longer true — only the event type and capacity constants live here. Misleads readers and would break any docs CI that denies broken links.
findings.recommendation: Change the intra-doc links to plain code spans (e.g. `` `BlockchainHandle` ``) or cross-crate links, and reword lib.rs:27-28 / services.rs:30 to say the handle and command enum live in neo-blockchain, this crate owns only BlockchainEvent + the capacity constants.

findings.detail: Three of the four service traits are unused scaffolding. There is no `impl BlockExecutor for ...`, `impl ConsensusService for ...`, or `impl NeoEngine for ...` anywhere in the workspace, the corresponding `with_block_executor`/`with_consensus`/`with_engine` builder methods are never called, and the Node slots are always `None`. The real block orchestration goes through neo-blockchain's BlockchainHandle and the real consensus loop is neo-consensus's own (unrelated) `ConsensusService` struct, which does not even depend on neo-runtime. The outcome.rs structs are self-described as 'Stage A placeholder' types. This is speculative vocabulary (YAGNI) carrying ExecutionPayload/ValidationResult/ExecutionOutcome/NeoEngine that nothing produces or consumes. Note the additional confusion risk: neo_runtime::ConsensusService (trait) collides by name with neo_consensus::ConsensusService (concrete struct).
findings.recommendation: Either wire at least one concrete implementor to prove the abstraction (preferred if these are the intended Engine-API seam), or trim to what is actually used (NetworkService) and reintroduce the others when a consumer lands — mirroring the earlier removal of the dead BlockchainHandle duplicate. If kept intentionally as a stable API seam, add a short note that they are not-yet-wired so reviewers do not treat them as live, and consider renaming to avoid the ConsensusService name clash.

findings.location: neo-runtime/Cargo.toml:24,26 (futures, parking_lot) and the tracing dependency
findings.detail: Declared dependencies `futures`, `parking_lot`, and `tracing` have zero usages in src/ (verified by grep: 0 hits each for `futures::`/`use futures`, `parking_lot::`, and any tracing macro). They add to the dependency graph and build time without being referenced. Additionally `tokio` is pulled in with workspace `features=["full"]` but the crate only uses `tokio::sync::broadcast` (one import) plus `#[tokio::test]`.
findings.recommendation: Drop the unused futures/parking_lot/tracing dependencies. If practical, depend on tokio with only the `sync` (and dev `macros`/`rt` for the test) features instead of the inherited `full` feature set to keep this leaf crate lean.

findings.detail: `assert!(outcome.ok || !outcome.ok)` is a tautology that is always true regardless of the value; the trailing comment even admits it is 'trivial'. It asserts nothing about behavior and gives a false sense of coverage for execute().
findings.recommendation: Replace with a real assertion on the default outcome (e.g. `assert!(!outcome.ok)` since ExecutionOutcome::default() has ok=false, or assert the block_hash/gas_consumed defaults), or remove the line and keep only the meaningful `.expect()` calls.

per_crate.overall: neo-error is a small (623 LoC), clean foundation crate (Layer 0) that owns the workspace-wide CoreError/CoreResult vocabulary. The layering rationale is well documented, the public API is fully doc-commented (#![warn(missing_docs)] is satisfied), and the message-flattening design (CoreError derives Clone + PartialEq + Eq, so every variant stores a String rather than a boxed source error) is a deliberate and correct trade-off for an error that must cross ~25 crate boundaries cheaply and be comparable in tests. The From<PrimitiveError> conversion is exhaustive and the cross-crate From-impl convention (impls live in owning crates, never here) is sound. The issues are not correctness-critical: a dead helper trait, lossy std-error conversions that discard diagnostic context, and an over-broad/duplicated constructor surface where several variants and constructors have zero callers. Nothing here is on the consensus path, so impact is low-to-medium and mostly about clarity and diagnostics.
strengths: Clean Layer-0 layering: depends only on neo-primitives + neo-io + thiserror, and the lib.rs doc explains exactly why CoreError lives here and why cross-crate From impls must live in owning crates (error.rs:472-487). Do not move these impls back here.

Message-flattening design is intentional and correct: CoreError derives Clone+PartialEq+Eq (error.rs:35), which forces String payloads instead of boxed sources; this is what makes CoreError cheap to pass across ~25 crates and comparable in tests. Do not 'improve' this by adding #[from] boxed source errors without understanding the Clone/Eq requirement.
Public API is fully documented and #![warn(missing_docs)]/#![deny(unsafe_code)] are honored (lib.rs:32-33).
From<PrimitiveError> is exhaustive over PrimitiveError's variants (error.rs:455-470) and uses a real match rather than a catch-all, so adding a PrimitiveError variant is a compile error here — good.
category()/is_retryable()/is_user_error() classification helpers are exhaustive matches with no wildcard arm (error.rs:403-422), so new variants force a compile-time decision.
The impl_error_from! macro is correctly re-exported from neo-io rather than duplicated, keeping the macro's single home (lib.rs:39-45).

findings.detail: ToNativeError / native_err is dead code. It is defined, blanket-impl'd, and publicly re-exported, but a workspace-wide grep finds zero callers and zero imports outside neo-error itself. Worse, its doc claims it 'eliminates the repetitive .map_err(CoreError::native_contract)? pattern found throughout native contract implementations' (error.rs:433-435), yet that pattern does not occur anywhere in the codebase (the only matches are the doc comment and the impl body). So the trait solves a problem the codebase does not have.
findings.recommendation: Delete the ToNativeError trait, its impl, and the lib.rs re-export. If a .native_err() ergonomic is genuinely wanted later, re-add it together with its first real call site.

findings.detail: These four std-error conversions bind the source as `_error` and discard it entirely, substituting a constant string (e.g. CoreError::type_conversion("string","integer") or invalid_data("invalid UTF-8 sequence")). The original error's message/position (e.g. ParseIntError kind, Utf8Error.valid_up_to()) is lost, which degrades diagnostics for any parse/decoding failure that propagates via `?`. Since CoreError variants already carry a String, preserving error.to_string() costs nothing and is consistent with how the impl_error_from! macro handles the other std conversions (error.rs:448-453, which DO call .to_string()).
findings.recommendation: Capture the source: e.g. `CoreError::type_conversion("string", "integer")` -> include `error.to_string()` in the message, or for UTF-8 use invalid_data(format!("invalid UTF-8 sequence: {error}")). Treat as 'needs care' rather than a blind change so the chosen variant/category stays stable.

findings.detail: PrimitiveError::InvalidData and the UTF-8 conversions funnel into CoreError::Invalid via invalid_data(), but CoreError::Invalid renders as `"Invalid: {message}"` (error.rs:38) whereas the source rendered as `"Invalid data: {message}"` (neo-primitives error.rs:16). There is no distinct InvalidData variant on CoreError, so the 'data' framing is silently dropped and the displayed text changes on conversion. Minor, but it makes log-grepping by error text inconsistent between layers.
findings.recommendation: Either accept the flattening explicitly (a one-line comment noting Invalid subsumes InvalidData) or add a dedicated InvalidData variant. Lowest-effort: document the intentional collapse so a future reader does not treat it as a bug.

findings.location: neo-error/src/error.rs:175-222 (invalid_format, invalid_data, invalid_argument) and the crypto family 247-286
findings.detail: invalid_format, invalid_data, and invalid_argument have byte-identical bodies (all produce Self::Invalid { message }). Likewise invalid_private_key/invalid_nep2_key/invalid_wif/scrypt/aes/invalid_password (error.rs:247-286) are six identical wrappers over Self::Cryptographic. These add API surface and a maintenance burden without adding type-level distinction (the resulting variant and Display are identical). Usage counts confirm several are near-dead (invalid_private_key=1, invalid_password=1, aes=2).
findings.recommendation: Keep one canonical constructor per variant (e.g. invalid / cryptographic) and drop the synonym helpers, OR if the call-site names are valued for readability, leave a short doc note that they are intentional aliases. Do not remove the two or three that have meaningful call counts without updating callers.

findings.location: neo-error/src/error.rs:104-106 (EndOfStream variant) and several zero-use constructors
findings.detail: CoreError::EndOfStream is never constructed anywhere in the workspace (grep finds zero sites). Constructors network(), system(), cryptographic(), type_conversion(), validation_failed(), configuration(), buffer_overflow(), runtime_error() also have zero external callers. The variant/constructor surface is broader than the code actually uses, which obscures which errors are real and live. Base58Decode has no constructor at all and is built via a struct literal at neo-wallets/src/key_pair.rs:390, an inconsistency with every other variant.
findings.recommendation: Trim genuinely unused constructors/variants, or — if they are intentionally provided as a stable public vocabulary for downstream crates — add a brief module note saying so. At minimum, add a Base58Decode constructor for consistency so callers stop using a raw struct literal.

findings.detail: The test module carries #[allow(dead_code)] on top of #[cfg(test)]. Test modules do not normally trigger dead_code on their #[test] fns, so this allow is either unnecessary (cargo-cult) or is masking a real unused helper that should be removed. It is a small smell in an otherwise tidy file.
findings.recommendation: Remove the #[allow(dead_code)]; if the build then warns, delete the actually-dead test helper rather than suppressing it.

cross_cutting_themes: DEAD/PARALLEL SUBSYSTEMS are the dominant theme — nearly every crate carries a complete, exported-but-never-wired abstraction left over from the pre-consolidation NeoSystem/actor architecture. Confirmed instances: neo-state-service entire verification pipeline (StateStore candidate cache, Verifier, StateServiceCommitHandlers no-op, SyntheticStateRootCalculator, StateRootCache, metrics) with the live MptStore unconsulted by RPC; neo-network TaskManagerService (315 LoC no-op) + ProtocolMessage/NetworkMessage typed wire layer (~380 LoC) never used by the manual on_message path; neo-mempool TransactionRouter + reverify/commit_block (no callers) + misnamed TransactionVerificationContext; neo-execution interop_descriptor/interop_parameter_descriptor (~400 LoC) + legacy consume_gas/check_gas/add_gas; neo-vm gas-metering subsystem + ~12 never-constructed VmError variants; neo-runtime BlockExecutor/ConsensusService/NeoEngine traits with zero implementors; neo-config global HardforkManager singleton + GenesisConfig; neo-primitives Fixed8 sidecar + dead VM-limit constants; neo-error ToNativeError trait. These obscure the real (small) public contract of each crate.

STRINGLY-TYPED ERRORS (Result<_,String>) persist in public/boundary APIs against the CONVENTIONS.md layered-error strategy (neo_error::CoreResult across crate boundaries). Confirmed in: neo-manifest (method_token, nef_file, contract_method/event_descriptor, contract_abi, wild_card_container, contract_permission_descriptor — ~8 public parsers), neo-serialization (BinarySerializer/JsonSerializer public APIs), neo-wallets (bip32, version.rs, WalletFactory trait), neo-system TxRouterHandle, neo-crypto NodeType::from_byte. Forces ad-hoc .map_err at every call site and weakens the typed-error guarantee.

STALE MODULE DOCUMENTATION referencing the dissolved neo-core or removed types — a direct artifact of the consolidation refactor. Confirmed in: neo-network lib.rs ('depends on neo-blockchain/neo-mempool/neo-core', 'Back-compat Stage F' legacy-actor section), neo-manifest lib.rs + manifest/mod.rs ('remain in neo_core', 'neo_core is canonical home'), neo-payloads validation.rs/verifiable_ext.rs ('live in neo-core'), neo-runtime lib.rs (4 broken intra-doc links to moved BlockchainHandle/BlockchainCommand). Misleads readers to a crate that no longer exists / must not be depended on.

BROKEN/FICTIONAL CRATE-LEVEL DOC EXAMPLES — the first thing an API reader sees references types that do not exist. Confirmed in: neo-config lib.rs (Settings::from_file/default_for_network — no Settings type, and it's JSON not TOML), neo-hsm lib.rs (HsmRuntime — no such type), neo-system cancellation_token doc contradicts impl (claims independent token, returns a shared clone). All are `ignore`d examples so doctests never caught them.

CRATE-WIDE BLANKET LINT ALLOWS that mask exactly the dead-code/unused-import issues these reviews surfaced, inconsistent across the 26 crates. Confirmed: neo-native-contracts (#![allow(dead_code/unused_imports/deprecated)] — the latter two verified no-ops today), neo-wallets (#![allow(dead_code/unused_imports)] contradicting #![warn(missing_docs)]), neo-oracle-service several neofs files (#![allow(dead_code)]), neo-error test-module #[allow(dead_code)]. Should be narrowed to per-item allows.

REINVENTED / DUPLICATED CONSENSUS-RELEVANT DECODERS that risk drift: the NEP-17 GAS AccountState balance decode exists 3x (neo-native-contracts gas_token read_gas_account, neo-mempool verification gas_balance_of, neo-rpc wallet_compat); Merkle root hand-rolled in neo-tee duplicating neo-crypto MerkleTree; block merkle/duplicate validators duplicated between neo-payloads and neo-blockchain; timestamp/primary-index header checks duplicated between neo-blockchain handlers (live, inline) and block_validation.rs (dead helpers).

i64 ARITHMETIC where C# uses BigInteger / unchecked wrapping on fee/economic paths — unreachable at current bounds but a latent divergence class needing differential tests, not blind changes: neo-native-contracts notary single_reward wrapping_mul, neo-tee FcfsWithGasCap network_fee*mult overflow, neo-primitives BigDecimal Mul decimals u8 overflow.

UNUSED DEPENDENCIES bloating build + dependency-audit surface, residue from the old architecture: neo-node (~dozen: mimalloc, prometheus, sysinfo, chrono, rand, url, zeroize, num_cpus, tracing-appender, async-trait + 4 optional neo-* crates), neo-consensus (neo-system, neo-network, async-trait), neo-config (uuid, dirs, toml), neo-runtime (futures, parking_lot, tracing), neo-tee (anyhow).

prioritized_actions.title: Fix reachable usize-underflow panic in RPC wallet fee selection
prioritized_actions.affected: neo-rpc/src/server/wallet_compat.rs:492 (find_paying_accounts; multi-output loop at :700, pre-check at :690)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed: `let mut i = ordered_accounts.len() - 1;` with no emptiness guard. The sum-across-all-outputs pre-check (:690) does not guarantee each per-output call leaves a non-empty list as balances drain. Reachable on attacker-influenced wallet inputs (sendmany/sendfrom). catch_unwind turns it into -32603 rather than a crash, but it is a real reachable panic. Guard the empty case (return InsufficientFunds) before `len()-1` and add a multi-output exhaustion test. No protocol bytes touched.

prioritized_actions.title: Fix BigDecimal::parse usize underflow on trailing-zero amounts (port C# trim-before-dot)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed: `while s.ends_with('0') { s.pop(); decimal_places -= 1; }` runs on a usize AFTER the '.' is removed, so '0.0'/'10.0'/'100.0'/'0.000' underflow. dev profile (overflow-checks+panic=abort) aborts; release wraps to usize::MAX -> garbage decimals scale -> silently wrong amount. Reachable from RPC wallet endpoints; only the Err path is caught, not the panic. C# Neo.BigDecimal.TryParse trims '0' BEFORE removing '.' with signed arithmetic. This is amount-parsing on a consensus-adjacent path -> port the C# algorithm and gate with a differential test against C# vectors ('0.0','10.0','100.0','1.230').

prioritized_actions.title: Remove inherent Transaction::size() that shadows Serializable::size() (wrong fee-per-byte + RPC size)
prioritized_actions.affected: neo-payloads/src/transaction/mod.rs:107-122 (inherent size); callers core.rs:246 (fee_per_byte) and json.rs:16 (to_json); feeds neo-mempool/src/pool_item.rs:80
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed: the private inherent size() omits the witnesses var-array entirely, omits the script var-int prefix, and sums the version field as part of `4+4` where the wire format uses 1 byte. Rust resolves `self.size()` to the inherent method, so fee_per_byte() (which feeds mempool fee ordering) and the RPC tx JSON `size` are both computed from an undersized value. Delete the inherent method (or make both callers use `<Self as Serializable>::size(self)`) and assert tx.fee_per_byte()/json size equal the serialized byte length including witnesses. Contained to in-crate use (method is private) but produces wrong mempool prioritization.

prioritized_actions.title: Fix NEP-2 verification-script salt to use CheckSig redeem script (C#/standard-wallet interop)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed: the helper emits SYSCALL + raw ASCII b"System.Crypto.CheckWitness" instead of the canonical PUSHDATA1+pubkey+SYSCALL+<4-byte LE hash of System.Crypto.CheckSig>. Both wrong syscall name AND wrong encoding, so the scrypt salt (script-hash address) diverges from C#. NEP-2 '6P...' keys are NOT interoperable with standard wallets; the round-trip test passes only because the same wrong helper is on both sides. KeyPair::get_verification_script (key_pair.rs:153) already does it correctly via Helper::signature_redeem_script. Replace the body with that call. Protocol-locked byte behavior -> lock with a published NEP-2 known-answer vector (differential test).

prioritized_actions.title: Wire TxRouterHandle to mempool+network (oracle responses silently discarded)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed: try_enqueue_preverify always returns Ok(()) and drops the tx. It is NOT dead — the oracle service submits a fully-signed oracle-response tx through it and then logs 'oracle response tx relayed', but nothing is relayed. Node already owns Arc<MemoryPool> + NetworkHandle, so the seam exists unwired. Either admit to mempool + broadcast (relay=true), or make the stub return Err so the oracle logs warn! instead of false success. Pair with an integration test asserting the tx reaches the mempool. Medium risk: touches relay path behavior, but no wire bytes.

prioritized_actions.title: Fix mempool capacity bound to cover total pool, not just verified queue
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed-class: capacity is enforced only against verified.len(); survivors of a persisted block are dumped into the unverified queue with no capacity check, so the pool can hold ~2x memory_pool_max_transactions (capacity verified + capacity unverified). C# RemoveOverCapacity loops over total Count and GetLowestFeeTransaction evicts the global lowest-fee item (preferring unverified). Weakens the DoS memory bound the setting exists to enforce. Track total occupancy, evict global lowest-fee preferring unverified, loop until total<=capacity. Differential test since it affects resource bounding. No wire bytes.

prioritized_actions.title: Bound the unbounded in-memory LedgerContext (OOM/DoS on a synced node)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed-class: LedgerContext accumulates the ENTIRE chain in RAM (full Block bodies + full Headers + hashes) with no eviction, monotonically growing per persisted block, while each block is also written durably and RPC reads cold data from the durable LedgerContract. Mainnet sync (millions of blocks) OOMs; an attacker feeding blocks accelerates it. insert_block also clones the whole Block. Bound with an LRU/ring of the last N blocks/headers (the lru crate is already a dep), keep height->hash, resolve bodies from the durable store on cold reads. No protocol bytes.

prioritized_actions.title: Enforce RpcServerConfig DoS/abuse limits on the jsonrpsee server (currently parsed but inert)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed-class: the server is built with Server::builder().build_from_tcp(...) using all defaults — none of max_request_body_size, max_concurrent_connections, max_batch_size, keep_alive/header timeouts, CORS, or rpc_user/rpc_pass auth are applied, and the fully-tested GovernorRateLimiter is re-exported but never invoked on any request path. For a node bindable to a non-loopback address this means no body cap, no batch-amplification cap, no per-IP rate limiting, and no auth even when configured — a false sense of protection. Wire the config into ServerBuilder + a tower/jsonrpsee middleware layer; mark any intentionally-deferred knobs explicitly. No protocol bytes; high operational value.

prioritized_actions.title: Make broadcast fan-out non-blocking so one slow peer can't stall the network command loop
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed-class: broadcast handlers `.await` per-peer bounded mpsc sends inside the single LocalNodeService command loop; a slow/malicious peer whose channel (cap 64) fills blocks the entire loop — stalling Start/ConnectPeer/DisconnectPeer and every other peer's broadcast + block/consensus relay. C# isolates per-RemoteNode mailboxes. Use try_send (log+drop to the slow peer on Full) or fan out via tokio::spawn/FuturesUnordered. Liveness/DoS fix; no wire bytes.

prioritized_actions.title: Make RPC getstateroot/getstateheight read the populated MptStore instead of the empty in-memory StateStore
prioritized_actions.affected: neo-rpc/src/server/rpc_server_state.rs:106-125; backed by neo-state-service StateStore vs MptStore (current_local_root_index/get_state_root)
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed-class: nothing in production calls try_add_state_root, so the in-memory StateStore cache the two handlers read is always empty; the real records live in MptStore (written by apply_block_changes). Net effect: a running node serves getstateheight as null and getstateroot as 'unknown' even after applying blocks. Fall back to (or read primarily from) MptStore::current_local_root_index/get_state_root; add an integration test applying a block and asserting getstateroot returns it. Part of the larger neo-state-service verification-pipeline decision (see that item).

prioritized_actions.title: Decide and wire-or-delete the neo-state-service verification pipeline
prioritized_actions.why: Confirmed-class: an entire parallel state-root subsystem (StateStore candidate cache, Verifier, commit handlers, SyntheticStateRootCalculator, StateRootCache, metrics) is public API with zero production callers (only its own tests) while only MptStore is live. Two parallel state-root subsystems where one is dormant is the single largest dead-flexibility cluster. Either wire the commit handlers into the persist pipeline to drive MptStore::apply_block_changes (the real MPT), or delete the verification half until a consumer exists. Directly enables the RPC getstateroot fix above. No protocol bytes.

prioritized_actions.title: Decide oracle data-fetch feature wiring (node ships an oracle service that cannot fetch)
prioritized_actions.affected: neo-oracle-service/Cargo.toml:62-66 (oracle/neofs-grpc features, default=[]); src/service/processing/url.rs:68-90; neo-node/Cargo.toml:91; neo-rpc/Cargo.toml:28,114
prioritized_actions.why: All HTTP/NeoFS fetching is behind the `oracle` feature which no consumer enables, and process_url has no not(feature) arm for https, so an https request falls through to ProtocolNotSupported. The node can verify/submit oracle signatures but cannot fetch any oracle data — either dead flexibility or a wiring bug. Decide intent: propagate the feature to neo-node/neo-rpc if fetching should work (add a build/test exercising the feature-on path), or document that the default node cannot fulfil requests. No protocol bytes.

prioritized_actions.title: Add differential test + align CheckMultisig error-vs-false classification with C#
prioritized_actions.why: On a verify error the Rust loop returns Err (faults the syscall), but C# CheckMultisig wraps the verify loop in catch(ArgumentException){return false} — so invalid-but-decodable pubkeys/sigs yield clean `false` in C#, while malformed DecodePoint bytes fault in BOTH. CheckMultisig sits on the witness-verification/consensus path, so 'returns false' vs 'engine FAULT' can change whether a tx/block verifies. PROTOCOL-LOCKED-adjacent: add a differential test covering invalid-but-decodable and malformed pubkeys, then map only the ArgumentException-class failures to Ok(false). Verify, do not blindly refactor.

prioritized_actions.title: Port full C# JSONPath grammar + DoS bounds for Oracle filter (consensus divergence risk)
prioritized_actions.why: The hand-rolled evaluator handles only $, ., .ident, [number], [*] and lacks recursive descent ($..), slices, unions, negative indices, quoted keys — and omits C#'s maxDepth=6/maxObjects=1024 DoS bounds. The filter comes from the on-chain OracleRequest (untrusted), so a filter valid in C# but rejected/expanded differently in Rust yields a different oracle response committed on-chain = consensus divergence; missing maxObjects is a DoS amplifier. parse_string also double-counts the closing quote for $['a']. PROTOCOL-LOCKED: port the full C# JPathToken grammar+guards and pin with C#-generated fixtures before any change. Until done, document the supported subset and the consensus risk.

prioritized_actions.title: Add NEF empty-script rejection + MaxItemSize cap on deserialize (deploy-path parity)
prioritized_actions.why: C# NefFile.Deserialize rejects empty scripts (`Script.Length==0` throws) and reads the script capped at MaxItemSize with a total-size verify; the Rust path reads with u32::MAX and has no empty-script or total-size check on read (MaxItemSize is checked only on serialize). Reachable on deploy/update via parse_nef_checked, which only guards the outer payload length. A NEF with valid magic+checksum but zero-length/oversized script is accepted where C# rejects. PROTOCOL-LOCKED wire behavior: read with ExecutionEngineLimits MaxItemSize cap, reject empty/oversized, gate with C# fixtures (empty-script + oversized-script).

prioritized_actions.title: Fix ContractAbi::get_method to return FIRST overload for any-arity (pcount=-1) lookup
prioritized_actions.affected: neo-manifest/src/manifest/contract_abi.rs:39-58 (get_method); reached by neo-execution helper.rs:264-271 and neo-oracle-service response.rs:109-113 (verify resolution)
prioritized_actions.why: C# returns Methods.FirstOrDefault(name) for pcount==-1, but the Rust HashMap inserts (name,-1)=>i for every method in order so a later duplicate-name overwrites and the lookup returns the LAST overload. Reachable: VERIFY_P_COUNT==-1 resolves a contract's `verify` method; a contract with multiple `verify` overloads (legal ABI) would resolve differently than C#. Use `methods.iter().find(name)` for pcount<0; only build the (name,len) map for pcount>=0. Differential test with two same-named overloads. Affects verify resolution -> risk=high.

prioritized_actions.title: Verify-ordering: move witness verification before mempool/consensus dedup poisoning
prioritized_actions.why: process_message calls mark_message_seen + update_last_seen_message over the UNSIGNED ExtensiblePayload content BEFORE any witness/signature verification (which happens later in per-type handlers). Since the dedup hash excludes the witness and the content is predictable, an attacker can precompute the hash, inject a forged garbage-witness payload first, and the genuine signed message is dropped as a duplicate; the forged message also skews last_seen/count_failed. Only safe if the relay layer runs ExtensiblePayload.Verify first — undocumented/unenforced. Either verify the witness at the top of process_message, or document+test the relay-seam precondition. Do not change the dedup hash bytes (protocol-locked) — fix is ordering/seam.

prioritized_actions.title: Wire mimalloc as global allocator or remove it (currently half-configured dead weight)
prioritized_actions.why: mimalloc is a direct dep (default-features=false, which disables the global-allocator override) but there is no #[global_allocator] anywhere, so the node uses the system allocator and pays the mimalloc build cost for zero benefit. A node binary normally installs mimalloc precisely for allocation-heavy block/VM workloads. Either add `#[global_allocator] static GLOBAL: mimalloc::MiMalloc = mimalloc::MiMalloc;` (enabling the right feature) if the perf intent is real, or drop the dep. Do not leave it half-wired.

prioritized_actions.title: Consolidate the triplicated NEP-17 GAS balance decode into one neo-native-contracts reader
prioritized_actions.why: Three independent copies of the same consensus-relevant AccountState decode (prefix-20 key, BinarySerializer::deserialize, Struct field 0 as_int) risk drifting apart.
